items.reason: UK shipping and trade rely on predictable international rules for collisions and salvage to minimize disputes, insurance costs, and delays; deletion would misalign UK law with global standards, imposing competitive disadvantages on British shipowners and exporters via legal uncertainties and higher risks that private contracts cannot reliably harmonize across jurisdictions without such institutional framework.

items.title: Resident Magistrates (Belfast) Act 1911
items.summary: The Resident Magistrates (Belfast) Act 1911 is an outdated statute establishing or regulating the positions and functions of resident magistrates specifically in Belfast, Northern Ireland.
items.reason: This 1911 Act is an archaic, hyper-local piece of legislation that imposes rigid, government-mandated judicial structures from over a century ago, likely superseded by modern unified justice systems across the UK and Northern Ireland. Retaining it clutters the legal code, raises compliance and interpretive costs for courts and citizens, distorts incentives in judicial administration by preserving obsolete roles, and exemplifies regulatory creep where old rules linger without delivering net benefits—private property and liberty are unaffected by its deletion, as contemporary laws adequately handle magistracy without this relic.

items.title: Perjury Act 1911
items.summary: The Perjury Act 1911 consolidates and simplifies laws punishing perjury in judicial proceedings, false statements on oath outside courts, false declarations for marriage, births/deaths, statutory declarations, vocational registrations, and related aiding/suborning, with penalties up to 7 years penal servitude.
items.reason: Deleting this would undermine the rule of law by removing deterrence against lying under oath in courts and official processes, leading to unreliable testimony, miscarried justice, unenforceable contracts, eroded property rights, and distorted incentives in dispute resolution—core institutions for liberty, markets, and prosperity that would collapse without credible enforcement; safeguards like corroboration minimise abuse while ensuring truthfulness in proceedings essential for individualism and economic order.

items.title: Municipal Elections (Corrupt and Illegal Practices) Act 1911
items.summary: Prohibits corrupt and illegal practices (e.g., bribery, treating, undue influence, impersonation) in municipal elections to ensure fair local voting.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave municipal elections unprotected from overt corruption, enabling vote-buying and coercion that distorts democratic competition, entrenches power monopolies, and elects unaccountable officials harming prosperity and liberty—outcomes not self-sustaining without such institutional safeguards against clear abuses, unlike free markets which thrive on voluntary exchange.

items.title: Merchant Shipping (Seamen's Allotment) Act 1911
items.summary: The Merchant Shipping (Seamen's Allotment) Act 1911 enables seamen on merchant ships to voluntarily allot portions of their wages to specified dependants (e.g., family), requiring shipowners or masters to deduct and remit these payments.
items.reason: This outdated mandate forces private shipowners to act as involuntary remittance agents, imposing administrative compliance costs, verification burdens, and potential disputes that distort labor contracts and raise shipping expenses—costs passed to consumers or reducing seamen's overall employment opportunities. Modern banking, direct deposits, and private transfer services (e.g., Western Union, apps) provide superior, voluntary alternatives without government coercion, freeing markets to innovate and reducing regulatory bloat that hampers maritime liberty and efficiency.

items.title: Public Libraries (Art Galleries in County Boroughs) (Ireland) Act 1911
items.summary: The Public Libraries (Art Galleries in County Boroughs) (Ireland) Act 1911 enabled or regulated the establishment of art galleries within public libraries in county boroughs in Ireland (then part of the UK).
items.reason: This 1911 legislation is obsolete: Ireland gained independence in 1922, county boroughs as defined no longer exist in the modern UK or Ireland, and public libraries/art galleries are governed by contemporary laws. Retaining it imposes unseen costs via legal clutter, complicating statute book navigation, increasing compliance/administrative burdens for lawyers and officials, and risking unintended misapplications in edge cases, without delivering any prosperity-enhancing benefits today.

items.title: Consolidated Fund (No. 1) Act 1920
items.summary: An Act authorizing the issue of money from the Consolidated Fund to cover government expenditure for the 1920-21 financial year.
items.reason: This is a historical, one-off appropriation act for expenditure in a specific long-past financial year; it no longer authorizes any current spending and serves no ongoing purpose. Keeping it on the books adds unnecessary clutter to the statute book, increasing legal complexity and administrative burden without benefit. Deleting it incurs no costs, avoids unseen consequences like perpetuating a bloated legislative corpus that distorts focus from active laws, and aligns with reducing government bloat to enhance liberty and efficiency.

items.title: National Health Insurance Act 1920
items.summary: The National Health Insurance Act 1920 extended compulsory national health insurance to deposit contributors, manual workers, and dependants, providing benefits including medical treatment, sickness and disablement pay, maternity grants, and medical certification, funded by tripartite contributions from workers, employers, and the state.
items.reason: This act mandates participation in a government-run insurance scheme, distorting healthcare markets by crowding out private provision, creating moral hazard (overuse of services), and imposing administrative costs that exceed voluntary market efficiencies. Unseen consequences include reduced incentives for personal savings and responsibility, path dependency toward full state monopolies like the NHS, stifled innovation and competition, and higher long-term costs as supply is constrained and prices inflated. Liberty and prosperity flourish through voluntary exchanges, not coerced redistribution.

items.title: Ejection (Suspensory Provisions) (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Ejection (Suspensory Provisions) (Scotland) Act 1920 was a temporary measure suspending ejection (eviction) procedures for certain tenants in Scotland, likely in response to post-WWI housing pressures under prior landholder acts.
items.reason: This act interferes with private property rights by suspending landlords' ability to eject non-paying or problematic tenants, distorting rental market incentives, discouraging property investment and maintenance, and contributing to long-term housing shortages and higher costs—unseen consequences that outweigh any short-term tenant protections, as free markets better allocate housing through voluntary contracts.

items.title: Savings Banks Act 1920
items.summary: The Savings Banks Act 1920 regulated trustee savings banks, non-profit institutions aimed at promoting savings among the working classes, establishing rules for their operation, management, and governance.
items.reason: This Act is obsolete, as trustee savings banks were privatized and merged into modern commercial banks (e.g., TSB into Lloyds) under subsequent legislation like the Trustee Savings Banks Act 1981 and financial services reforms. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: legal bloat confuses statute books, risks misinterpretation in disputes over historical assets or trusts, diverts parliamentary resources from relevant deregulation, and perpetuates a statist model of savings promotion that distorted private incentives—better replaced by market-driven banking where individuals freely save via competitive institutions without government oversight.

items.title: Profiteering (Amendment) Act 1920
items.summary: The Profiteering (Amendment) Act 1920 amends the 1919 Profiteering Act, imposing price controls on essential goods to prevent excessive profits during post-WWI shortages.
items.reason: Price controls distort market signals, discouraging production and supply of goods as producers cannot cover costs or earn profits, leading to shortages, black markets, queues, and misallocation of resources. Unseen costs include reduced investment, innovation, and overall prosperity, as liberty and private property rights are undermined; historical evidence shows such interventions fail to sustainably lower prices or help the poor without harming incentives.

items.title: Tramways (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act 1920
items.summary: The Tramways (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act 1920 permitted tramway operators to temporarily increase passenger fares in response to post-World War I economic pressures.
items.reason: This century-old temporary measure exemplifies government price intervention, distorting market incentives for transport pricing and private property rights. Even if largely obsolete, retaining it clogs the statute books, sets precedent for regulatory meddling, creates compliance burdens, and hinders competition and innovation in modern transport sectors by implying state oversight over fares is normative—unseen costs include reduced entrepreneurial entry and efficiency losses outweighing any archaic 'protection'.

items.title: Representation of the People Act 1920
items.summary: The Representation of the People Act 1920 extended the right to vote to all men over 21 and women over 30 meeting property qualifications, significantly broadening the electorate beyond previous restrictions.
items.reason: Deleting this foundational suffrage legislation would revert voting rights to pre-1920 restrictions, disenfranchising vast numbers of citizens and concentrating power among a property-owning elite. This would undermine individual liberty, reduce democratic accountability over government (essential for limiting state overreach as per Hayek's emphasis on institutions checking power), and risk elite capture that stifles prosperity through unaccountable policies. Broad franchise, despite risks of majority overreach, fosters the institutional competition needed for liberty and wealth creation; without it, citizens would be worse off with less voice against coercive institutions.

items.title: Imperial War Museum Act 1920
items.summary: The Imperial War Museum Act 1920 establishes a government-appointed Board of Trustees as a body corporate to manage the Imperial War Museum, vesting artifacts in it, granting powers to acquire/dispose of property and objects (with Treasury consent), appointing staff, and funding operations from parliamentary moneys, while exempting it from Charitable Trusts Acts.
items.reason: This Act institutionalizes taxpayer funding and bureaucratic oversight (e.g., Treasury consents for land, sales, appointments) for a public museum, diverting private wealth via taxes to a cultural institution that could be sustained voluntarily through donations, private enterprise, or philanthropy. Unseen costs include distorted incentives for private collectors/historians, inefficiency from government management lacking market competition, and opportunity costs of funds better used by individuals for productive investments or preferred causes; private museums thrive without such mandates, preserving history more dynamically.

items.title: Increase of Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Act 1920
items.summary: The Increase of Rent and Mortgage Interest (Restrictions) Act 1920 imposed controls on rent increases and mortgage interest rates for certain residential properties, primarily to protect tenants from post-WWI inflation and housing shortages.
items.reason: Rent controls exemplify regulatory failure: they distort price signals, discouraging new housing supply, incentivizing landlord neglect, fostering shortages, black markets, and misallocation—hurting the poor most by reducing affordable options. Unseen costs include stifled investment, perpetuated scarcity, and barriers to mobility, undermining liberty, property rights, and prosperity; markets self-regulate supply-demand far better without such interventions.

items.title: Finance Act 1920
items.summary: The Finance Act 1920 imposes and adjusts various taxes including increased customs and excise duties on spirits, beer, wine, tobacco, and medicines; introduces excise duties on mechanically propelled vehicles; sets income tax at 6 shillings (30%) with super-tax on higher incomes and personal allowances/deductions; raises stamp duties; continues and increases excess profits duty to 60%; introduces 5% corporation profits tax on businesses; repeals land values duties; and includes provisions on national debt and other fiscal matters.
items.reason: This act substantially increases tax burdens across consumption, income, business profits, and transactions to fund post-WWI debt and government spending, distorting incentives for production, investment, and trade (contra Hayek's price signals and Mises' calculation). Excess profits duty and corporation profits tax punish success and impose double taxation, reducing capital formation and entrepreneurship (Friedman). High excise/customs duties on goods like alcohol create monopolies/black markets, raise costs, and harm the poor disproportionately. Compliance/administrative costs are unseen deadweight losses; progressive super-tax encourages capital flight/emigration. Repeal of land values duties is positive, but overall, the act's interventionist fiscal expansion erodes private property, liberty, and prosperity without ensuring better institutions for outcomes like poverty reduction.

items.title: County Councils Association Expenses (Amendment) Act 1920
items.summary: An amendment act from 1920 regulating the expenses of the County Councils Association, a body representing county councils; full text only available as PDF, indicating obscurity and likely obsolescence.
items.reason: This 104-year-old minor administrative amendment perpetuates bureaucratic bloat, imposing unseen costs like maintenance in legal databases, potential for outdated funding claims on taxpayers, and distraction from simplifying governance. Deletion eliminates compliance confusion and opportunity costs without harming liberty or property, as private associations can self-fund via voluntary means, aligning with incentives for efficiency over state-mandated expense rules.

items.title: Merchant Shipping (Amendment) Act 1920 (repealed 1.1.1996)
items.summary: Amends section 659 of the Merchant Shipping Act 1894 to extend the power of His Majesty by Order in Council to fix unlimited annual or other sums payable from the General Lighthouse Fund to members of the general lighthouse authority for England and Wales, overriding prior statutory limits.
items.reason: This act removes statutory caps on payments to lighthouse authority members from light dues (a de facto tax on shipping), enabling potential rent-seeking and inflated compensation without market discipline. Unseen consequences include higher dues passed to shipowners, raising shipping costs, distorting trade incentives, reducing UK maritime competitiveness, and diverting funds from safety infrastructure to bureaucratic payouts. Prior limits provided a check; deletion reverts to them without harming safety, as private markets historically provided lighthouses effectively.

items.title: Veterinary Surgeons Act (1881) Amendment Act 1920
items.summary: Amendment to the Veterinary Surgeons Act 1881, updating regulations on veterinary surgeons' registration, qualifications, practice restrictions, and the role of the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons in licensing the profession.
items.reason: This legislation reinforces government-backed occupational licensing for veterinarians, creating barriers to entry that limit supply, drive up costs for animal care (impacting farmers, pet owners, and food prices), and protect incumbents at the expense of competition. Unseen consequences include stifled innovation in treatments, reduced services in rural areas, and distortion of educational incentives toward RCVS-approved paths only, preventing market-driven quality assurance via reputation, private certification, and consumer choice.

items.title: Harbours, Docks, and Piers (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act 1920
items.summary: The Harbours, Docks, and Piers (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act 1920 permitted a temporary increase in charges at UK harbours, docks, and piers, likely in response to post-WWI economic pressures.
items.reason: This outdated 1920 Act exemplifies government price intervention, distorting market signals for port services and creating uncertainty for private investment in infrastructure. Even if rarely invoked today, retaining it perpetuates regulatory clutter, invites future misuse for price controls, reduces competition by shielding operators from market discipline, and imposes unseen compliance costs on businesses, hindering efficient allocation of resources in a vital sector for UK trade and prosperity.

items.title: Ecclesiastical Tithe Rentcharge (Rates) Act 1920
items.summary: The Ecclesiastical Tithe Rentcharge (Rates) Act 1920 adjusts the rating (local property taxation) of ecclesiastical tithe rentcharges, which are historical perpetual charges on land payable to the Church of England.
items.reason: This 1920 Act perpetuates regulation of an archaic feudal remnant (tithe rentcharges, largely redeemed or obsolete post-1936 Tithe Act), imposing unseen compliance costs, legal complexity, and potential distortions in church land incentives or property markets; general modern property tax laws suffice, and retention clogs the statute book without enhancing liberty or prosperity.

items.title: War Pensions Act 1920
items.summary: An Act amending prior War Pensions Acts (1915-1919) to refine administration of WWI pensions and grants: re-transfers non-WWI powers to original departments, defines 'present war', enables district committees, enhances ministerial oversight for economy and accountability, restores forfeited pensions, grants statutory rights to widows/children, exempts from stamp duty, and makes interpretive changes.
items.reason: This Act expands bureaucratic machinery (ministerial powers, local committees, inquiries, finance controls) for state-funded WWI pensions, imposing taxpayer costs and administrative overhead with unseen consequences like inefficiency, mismanagement risks (necessitating interventions), and precedent for perpetual government welfare expansion over private charity/insurance. As a 1920 law for long-deceased beneficiaries, it now litters statute books without purpose, distorting incentives via ongoing public expenditure and reducing liberty by entrenching state dependency.

items.title: Bank Notes (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: Amends law on payment locations for bank notes issued by Irish banks of issue: pre-Act notes payable only at head office in Ireland; post-Act notes at expressed place; repeals 1828 and 1830 Acts requiring payment at issue place or Dublin.
items.reason: Obsolete 1920 legislation post-Irish partition (1922); no UK-jurisdictional 'banks of issue in Ireland' exist today (modern notes by central banks); keeping it creates statutory clutter, risks legal confusion, distorts incentives by preserving irrelevant mandates on private contracts, with zero prosperity benefits as private banknotes are historical relic.

items.title: Public Libraries (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: Consolidates and amends previous enactments to enable and regulate the provision of free public libraries by local authorities in Ireland, funded via local rates (taxes).
items.reason: Compels local taxation to fund government-run libraries, distorting incentives by crowding out private, market-driven alternatives like subscription libraries or charitable provision; unseen costs include bureaucratic inefficiencies, reduced innovation in knowledge dissemination, and resources diverted from productive private uses, harming overall prosperity without ensuring better outcomes for the poor than voluntary markets would.

items.title: Sheriffs (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Sheriffs (Ireland) Act 1920 (c. 26) regulated the appointment, duties, and operations of sheriffs in Ireland during the period of UK governance before Irish partition and independence.
items.reason: Enacted in 1920 amid Irish partition, this legislation is obsolete post-1922 independence of the Irish Free State (now Republic of Ireland) and applies only notionally to Northern Ireland where sheriff roles have been superseded by modern judicial and enforcement structures. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter confuses practitioners and citizens, risks erroneous citations in NI courts, diverts minor administrative resources for maintenance/amendments, and perpetuates historical relics without benefiting liberty or prosperity—private property and incentives are unaffected by its absence, as core sheriff functions evolved organically without it.

items.title: Nauru Island Agreement Act 1920
items.summary: The Nauru Island Agreement Act 1920 (c. 27) ratifies an agreement between the UK, Australia, and New Zealand for the joint administration of Nauru Island, a former mandate territory rich in phosphates, following World War I.
items.reason: Nauru achieved independence in 1968, rendering this Act obsolete with no ongoing impact on UK citizens, economy, or institutions. Keeping defunct colonial-era laws clutters the statute book, raises unseen costs in legal research, compliance, and maintenance, distorts incentives for efficient governance, and perpetuates unnecessary state intrusion into historical irrelevancies without achieving any liberty-enhancing outcomes.

items.title: Gas Regulation Act 1920
items.summary: The Gas Regulation Act 1920 regulates gas supply undertakings by imposing controls on prices, quality standards, accounting practices, and operational obligations for gas companies.
items.reason: This act exemplifies early 20th-century economic regulation that distorts market signals through price caps and mandated standards, fostering monopolistic inefficiencies, stifling innovation in supply and technology, and imposing unseen compliance costs that raise consumer prices and reduce service quality over time; competition and private contracts would better ensure reliable, affordable gas without government intervention.

items.title: Overseas Trade (Credits and Insurance) Act 1920
items.summary: The Overseas Trade (Credits and Insurance) Act 1920 empowers the government to provide credits and insurance guarantees for overseas trade, facilitating exports by assuming risks typically borne by private markets.
items.reason: This legislation institutionalizes government intervention in trade finance, distorting market incentives by subsidizing exporters with taxpayer-backed insurance and credits; it creates moral hazard (encouraging riskier deals), crowds out private insurers and lenders, fosters dependency on state support rather than market discipline, and imposes unseen costs through bureaucratic inefficiencies, misallocated resources, and potential cronyism benefiting large firms over broad prosperity—all contrary to wealth creation via liberty and private property.

items.title: Coinage Act 1920
items.summary: The Coinage Act 1920 establishes standards for the weight, composition, design, and fineness of UK coins, defines legal tender, regulates minting by the Royal Mint, and prohibits counterfeiting and unauthorized coin production.
items.reason: British citizens would be worse off without this legislation due to the absence of standardized legal tender definitions, enabling rampant counterfeiting, disputes over coin authenticity and value in transactions, and disruption of everyday small-value exchanges reliant on physical cash. It achieves reliable coinage as a trusted medium of exchange through clear institutional rules enforced by the state, preventing Gresham's Law chaos where bad money drives out good without such framework; private alternatives could emerge but would lack immediate stability absent gradual denationalization.

items.title: Unemployment Insurance Act 1920
items.summary: The Unemployment Insurance Act 1920 extended compulsory state-administered unemployment insurance to a broader range of manual workers, trades, and industries, funded by tripartite contributions from employees, employers, and the government.
items.reason: This compulsory scheme distorts labor markets by subsidizing unemployment (moral hazard, prolonging job searches), imposes administrative costs and bureaucracy, crowds out private insurance and mutual aid societies, raises payroll taxes that reduce wages and hiring incentives, and fosters dependency culture; unseen consequences include higher structural unemployment, reduced work ethic, business emigration, and stifled prosperity as resources are diverted from productive investment—outcomes classical liberals warned against, with no evidence it sustainably aids the poor without harming liberty and growth.

items.title: Restoration of Order in Ireland Act 1920
items.summary: The Restoration of Order in Ireland Act 1920 granted emergency powers to impose martial law, courts-martial, curfews, and internment without trial in designated parts of Ireland to suppress IRA insurgency and restore order during the Irish War of Independence.
items.reason: This obsolete 1920 emergency legislation, tied to a historical conflict resolved by partition and Irish independence, lingers on the statute books with no modern relevance. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: cluttering the legal system, risking revival for domestic suppression of dissent, distorting incentives toward authoritarianism over liberty, and eroding trust in institutions without achieving any current benefits, as targeted modern laws like the Terrorism Act 2000 already address security needs.

items.title: Appropriation Act 1920
items.summary: The Appropriation Act 1920 (c. 32) authorises the issue of sums from the Consolidated Fund for government services in the fiscal years ending 31 March 1920 and 1921, primarily post-WWI expenditures totaling around £1.25 billion (in period terms).
items.reason: This century-old act's spending authorisations have been fully executed or superseded by subsequent legislation. Retaining it clutters the statute book, imposes unseen maintenance costs on legal systems, and symbolically entrenches the precedent of unchecked government expansion, distorting incentives for fiscal restraint and crowding out private investment without delivering ongoing benefits.

items.title: Maintenance Orders (Facilities for Enforcement) Act 1920
items.summary: The Maintenance Orders (Facilities for Enforcement) Act 1920 enables reciprocal enforcement of maintenance orders (for spousal or dependant support) between courts in England/Ireland and other parts of the British Empire/Dominions/Protectorates, including registration, provisional orders, confirmations, and procedural mechanisms via the Secretary of State and governors.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave British dependants unable to enforce court-ordered maintenance against obligors who relocate within reciprocal Commonwealth jurisdictions, fostering evasion of private legal obligations, increasing family poverty and state welfare burdens, and eroding cross-border rule of law essential for upholding property rights in judgments—without it, the institution of enforceable family support fails, harming vulnerable individuals and overall stability more than the minimal bureaucratic costs impose.

items.title: Duplicands of Feu-duties (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: An Act amending the Feudal Casualties (Scotland) Act 1914 to cap 'duplicands' (double feu-duties) payable on entry of heirs or periodical occasions at one year's feu-duty (either over and above or inclusive), reducing burdens on vassals under Scotland's feudal land tenure system.
items.reason: This legislation perpetuates Scotland's obsolete feudal tenure system, which undermines clear private property rights through perpetual rents and casualties, distorting incentives for land use, improvement, and transfer. Though it mitigates superior abuses by capping payments, it sustains institutional complexity and legal uncertainty, with unseen costs in administrative burdens and reduced economic efficiency. Full abolition (as achieved in 2000) eliminates these distortions, promoting liberty and prosperity without need for such patchwork reforms.

items.title: Representation of the People (No. 2) Act 1920
items.summary: The Representation of the People (No. 2) Act 1920 established key provisions for UK parliamentary elections, including enabling women to stand as candidates, introducing proxy and postal voting for the disabled and absent servicemen, refining voter registration and qualifications, and improving electoral procedures.
items.reason: Deleting this legislation would dismantle foundational electoral institutions ensuring broad franchise access, candidate eligibility, and accommodations for voting, leading to disenfranchisement, unfair elections, and reduced government accountability. Britons would be worse off without these rules, as voluntary private arrangements could not reliably replicate impartial election conduct, eroding the democratic checks essential for protecting individual liberty and property rights against state overreach—outcomes later acts build upon but still depend on this framework.

items.title: Pensions (Increase) Act 1920
items.summary: The Pensions (Increase) Act 1920 provides a mechanism for periodic increases in certain public sector pensions (e.g., civil servants, teachers, police) payable from public funds, linked to rises in the cost of living, with provisions for Treasury approval.
items.reason: This act institutionalizes automatic upward adjustments to government pensions funded by taxation, creating perpetual fiscal commitments that distort incentives by rewarding public sector employment regardless of productivity. Unseen costs include higher taxes or debt crowding out private investment, inflating public spending, encouraging overstaffing and inefficiency in government, and reducing overall economic growth as resources are locked into unproductive entitlements rather than market-driven uses.

items.title: Telegraph (Money) Act 1920
items.summary: The Telegraph (Money) Act 1920 (c. 37) authorizes the raising of money by the Treasury for the purpose of telegraph works undertaken by the Postmaster-General, specifically for extending and improving telegraph services.
items.reason: This 1920 legislation funded state-run telegraph infrastructure, which is entirely obsolete in the modern era of digital telecommunications. Keeping it on the books imposes unseen costs: cluttering the legal system, increasing administrative burdens for legal research and compliance, and distorting incentives by preserving outdated government spending authorizations that no longer serve any purpose. Private markets have long supplanted state telegraphs with superior innovations; deletion would simplify law, reduce regulatory bloat, and free resources without harming citizens, as no current benefits accrue from it.

items.title: Resident Magistrates (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Resident Magistrates (Ireland) Act 1920 was a UK Public General Act (c. 38) concerning the appointment and role of resident magistrates in Ireland during the period leading up to partition.
items.reason: This 1920 legislation is obsolete following Irish partition in 1921 and the independence of the Republic of Ireland in 1922; Northern Ireland's judicial system has evolved under modern laws like the Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002 and county court reforms, rendering resident magistrates defunct. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute books, risks legal confusion or erroneous citations in NI courts, distorts institutional clarity, and perpetuates bureaucratic inertia without achieving any current public good, as no British citizens rely on it for justice or liberty.

items.title: Merchant Shipping (Scottish Fishing Boats) Act 1920 (repealed 1.1.1996)
items.summary: Extends Part IV (regulating fishing boats) of the Merchant Shipping Act 1894 to Scotland with local modifications (e.g., Edinburgh Gazette, 'burgh' for 'borough'), requires Secretary for Scotland's consent for certain orders, and repeals Scotland-specific exceptions in prior law.
items.reason: This 1920 act extends outdated regulatory burdens on fishing boat registration, manning, and operations to Scotland, imposing compliance costs, administrative hurdles, and incentive distortions that raise barriers to entry, increase fish prices, and stifle competition. Unseen consequences include reduced innovation in safety (better handled by private insurance/reputation) and favoritism toward incumbents; deletion allows market-driven efficiency and liberty without harming prosperity.

items.title: Coal Mines (Emergency) Act 1920
items.summary: The Coal Mines (Emergency) Act 1920 granted the government temporary emergency powers to control coal mines and ensure production during shortages, primarily in response to post-WWI coal crises.
items.reason: Retaining this 1920 emergency legislation imposes ongoing costs by preserving government intervention powers in private industry, distorting incentives for investment in energy markets, creating uncertainty for property owners, and setting precedents for future nationalizations that reduce supply, raise costs, and hinder innovation—unseen consequences outweigh any historical emergency benefits, as markets and private property reliably provide better long-term energy security without such distortions.

items.title: Post Office and Telegraph Act 1920
items.summary: The Post Office and Telegraph Act 1920 empowers the government-run Post Office to manage and regulate postal and telegraph services, including property acquisition and operational powers, maintaining a state monopoly on these communications.
items.reason: This act entrenches a government monopoly on postal and telegraph services, distorting market incentives, stifling private innovation, and raising costs through lack of competition—unseen consequences include slower technological progress (e.g., delayed telecom advancements) and reduced prosperity, as private enterprise would deliver better, cheaper services, aligning with historical privatizations that improved outcomes.

items.title: Census Act 1920
items.summary: The Census Act 1920 empowers the government to conduct periodic censuses in Great Britain for statistical population data (e.g., demographics, occupations, housing), with parliamentary oversight, frequency limits (at least 5 years apart), privacy penalties, and provisions for administration, local censuses, and Scotland.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave the UK without reliable, comprehensive, standardized population statistics, which are essential for minimal government functions like electoral boundaries, defense planning, and basic service allocation; private entities face insurmountable coordination and free-rider problems to achieve nationwide, unbiased coverage voluntarily at similar scale and accuracy, making citizens worse off by impairing informed decision-making in both public and private spheres without evident high unintended costs beyond routine administrative expenses and compulsion, which is narrowly targeted and infrequently applied.

items.title: Census (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Census (Ireland) Act 1920 (c. 42) made provision for the taking of a census in Ireland, scheduled for 1921.
items.reason: This legislation is obsolete following the partition of Ireland in 1921, with the Republic of Ireland now independent and conducting its own censuses, while Northern Ireland's census functions are governed by subsequent legislation such as the Census Act (Northern Ireland) 1969. Retaining it imposes unseen costs including legal clutter, unnecessary complexity in the statute books, potential for misinterpretation in legal research, and symbolic regulatory bloat that distracts from modern, effective institutions without delivering any ongoing benefits to prosperity or liberty.

items.title: Firearms Act 1920 (repealed 8.11.1995)
items.summary: The Firearms Act 1920 requires firearm certificates for purchasing, possessing, using, or carrying firearms and ammunition, granted by police if no danger to public safety; regulates dealers, prohibits sales to minors, drunk/insane persons, and criminals; bans certain weapons; includes exemptions for professions, clubs, and specific uses; and imposes penalties and registration requirements.
items.reason: Retaining this act imposes bureaucratic costs (fees, police discretion, appeals, record-keeping) that burden individuals and dealers, distorting incentives for legitimate activities like self-defense, hunting, and sport shooting, while failing to deter criminals who bypass laws, creating black markets and empowering state overreach; unseen consequences include reduced personal liberty, foregone economic activity in firearms trades, and no proven net safety gain as institutions prioritize permission over property rights, harming prosperity and individualism.

items.title: Fertilisers (Temporary Control of Export) Act 1920
items.summary: The Fertilisers (Temporary Control of Export) Act 1920 imposes temporary government controls on the export of fertilisers to ensure domestic supply.
items.reason: This century-old 'temporary' export restriction violates free trade and private property rights, distorting market incentives for fertiliser production and allocation; it risks shortages, higher costs, reduced supply incentives, and administrative burdens, with unseen consequences outweighing any outdated crisis rationale, as markets efficiently ration via prices without state intervention.

items.title: Public Libraries (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Public Libraries (Scotland) Act 1920 requires every county and burgh council in Scotland to provide a free public library service, funded by local rates (taxes).
items.reason: Mandating government provision of libraries compels taxation and creates bureaucracy, distorting incentives for private libraries, subscriptions, or digital alternatives that could better meet demand through voluntary exchange. Unseen costs include inefficient resource allocation, potential political censorship of materials, and crowding out innovation in information markets—evident in the rise of private and online libraries today. Without this Act, market competition would supply library services more efficiently, enhancing liberty and prosperity.

items.title: Dangerous Drugs Act 1920
items.summary: The Dangerous Drugs Act 1920 regulates the import, export, manufacture, possession, sale, and distribution of narcotic drugs such as opium, cocaine, and morphine, requiring licenses and prohibiting unauthorised handling.
items.reason: This act exemplifies regulatory overreach that criminalises voluntary adult transactions, creating black markets, cartel violence, impure adulterated drugs leading to overdoses, massive enforcement costs diverting resources from productive uses, and incarceration that distorts labour markets without reducing drug use (as evidenced by persistent consumption despite century-long prohibition). Unseen consequences include eroded civil liberties, police corruption incentives, and stifled medical innovation in drug therapies; private property and individual choice would foster safer, cheaper supply via market competition and personal responsibility, aligning with Friedman's critique of prohibition's failures.

items.title: Ministry of Food (Continuance) Act 1920
items.summary: The Ministry of Food (Continuance) Act 1920 extended the wartime Ministry of Food, originally established for rationing and price controls during WWI, into peacetime operations.
items.reason: Government control over food production, distribution, and pricing distorts market incentives, creates shortages, encourages black markets, raises costs for consumers, and stifles innovation in agriculture and trade. Post-war continuance perpetuated interventionist institutions that hinder private property rights and voluntary exchange, with unseen costs far outweighing any purported stability, as free markets have historically proven superior at ensuring food abundance and affordability.

items.title: Indemnity Act 1920
items.summary: The Indemnity Act 1920 (c. 48) provided legal indemnity to Crown servants, military personnel, and others for actions taken under wartime emergency powers (primarily the Defence of the Realm Act) from 4 August 1914 to 31 August 1920, protecting them from civil or criminal liability for any resulting breaches of law.
items.reason: This is a historical, one-off indemnity for past wartime actions, now fully obsolete and spent with no ongoing application or enforcement. Keeping it on the statute books creates dead-weight clutter, confuses legal researchers and practitioners with irrelevant text, incurs minor public costs in maintenance and digitization, and symbolically entrenches past government overreach without countervailing benefits—contrary to principles of clear, minimal institutions that foster liberty and efficiency.

items.title: Blind Persons Act 1920
items.summary: The Blind Persons Act 1920 requires local authorities to maintain registers of blind persons in their areas and to make arrangements for their welfare, including employment assistance.
items.reason: This paternalistic mandate imposes coercive taxpayer funding and bureaucratic registration on local authorities, distorting incentives for individual self-reliance and private charity. Unseen costs include administrative overhead, dependency culture, and crowding out more efficient voluntary organizations like the RNIB, which innovate without government compulsion. Liberty and prosperity flourish when aid is voluntary, not decreed.

items.title: War Emergency Laws (Continuance) Act 1920
items.summary: The War Emergency Laws (Continuance) Act 1920 extended the duration of temporary emergency powers and regulations enacted during World War I, such as controls on resources, trade, and civil liberties.
items.reason: Post-war emergency laws distort markets by imposing government controls that reduce supply, create shortages, foster black markets, and infringe on private property and individual liberty—core prerequisites for wealth creation per Mises, Hayek, and Friedman. Retaining this act perpetuates bureaucratic inertia, enables future political abuse of outdated powers, and signals to entrepreneurs and investors that arbitrary interventions linger, stifling prosperity and individualism. Unseen costs include foregone innovation and capital flight as incentives remain warped decades after the emergency.

items.title: Mining Industry Act 1920
items.summary: Establishes Mines Department under Board of Trade for central oversight of mining; temporary (to 1922) coal export/price/wage controls; mandates joint owner-worker pit/district/area/national committees for safety, output, disputes, remuneration schemes enforceable by government; compulsory 1d/ton welfare fund for colliery workers' social conditions; stats collection, drainage schemes, metalliferous mine regulations.
items.reason: This act imposes bureaucratic centralization, temporary but precedent-setting price/export controls that distort incentives, reduce supply, and invite shortages/black markets; forces joint governance committees undermining private property rights and efficient management via compelled info-sharing, inspections, and enforceable recommendations; levies output-based tax for government-directed welfare, crowding out wages/private charity and creating dependency; unseen costs include deterred investment, slowed innovation, industry emigration, and misallocated resources, preventing market-driven prosperity in mining.

items.title: Duchy of Lancaster Act 1920
items.summary: The Duchy of Lancaster Act 1920 updates the administration of the monarch's private Duchy of Lancaster estate by expanding investment powers for its funds (aligning with Trustee Act 1893), allowing mining leases up to 99 years, and establishing the Duchy solicitor as a corporation sole with enhanced legal capacities, while repealing outdated provisions.
items.reason: This legislation enables more efficient management of the Duchy's substantial private assets (valued in billions), allowing modern investments and long-term leases that maximize returns on private property. Deleting it would revert to archaic restrictions (e.g., 1855 Act limits), distorting incentives for productive use, reducing wealth generation from these lands, and potentially increasing taxpayer burden if lower private royal income heightens reliance on public funds for official duties—outcomes contrary to liberty, property rights, and prosperity principles.

items.title: Ready Money Football Betting Act 1920
items.summary: The Ready Money Football Betting Act 1920 prohibits cash (ready money) betting on football match results, allowing only credit betting, to curb street betting and associated social issues like absenteeism and disorder.
items.reason: This paternalistic restriction on voluntary adult exchanges violates liberty and private property rights, driving betting underground into black markets with higher risks, loan sharking from credit dependencies, and enforcement costs. It fails to reduce gambling overall—demand persists, supply distorts, creating monopolies for credit bookies—while unseen consequences include corruption, selective policing, and no proven net benefit to prosperity or the poor, as politicians' protective rhetoric masks institutional failures.

items.title: Jurors (Enrolment of Women) (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Jurors (Enrolment of Women) (Scotland) Act 1920 enabled the enrolment and eligibility of women to serve as jurors in Scotland.
items.reason: British citizens, particularly women, would be worse off without this legislation as it would revert to excluding half the population from jury service, resulting in unrepresentative juries, reduced impartiality in trials, and weakened rule of law—core institutions protecting individual liberty and property rights. This act achieves equal civic participation without which systemic bias would persist, as voluntary or common law measures historically failed to extend such rights broadly.

items.title: Seeds Act 1920
items.summary: The Seeds Act 1920 regulates the marketing, labelling, testing, and sale of agricultural seeds to ensure quality standards and prevent fraud on farmers.
items.reason: This legislation imposes government-mandated standards and testing regimes that raise compliance costs for seed producers, increasing seed prices and reducing affordability for farmers, especially smallholders. It distorts market incentives by creating barriers to entry for innovative or low-cost seed varieties, favouring large incumbents prone to regulatory capture and stifling competition. Unseen consequences include reduced agricultural innovation, supply constraints, and higher food costs passed to consumers. Private mechanisms—reputation, warranties, third-party certifications, and existing fraud laws—would better ensure quality through voluntary exchange, fostering liberty, efficiency, and prosperity without state intervention.

items.title: Emergency Powers Act 1920 (Repealed)
items.summary: The Emergency Powers Act 1920 empowers the government to declare a state of emergency for threats to public safety, health, or essential supplies (e.g., food, water, fuel), granting the executive broad powers to issue regulations including requisitions, restrictions on movement, and controls on trade.
items.reason: Grants vague, sweeping discretionary powers to the executive, bypassing Parliament and rule of law, leading to unintended consequences like economic distortions (rationing reduces supply and incentives), property rights violations, and mission creep into permanent controls (e.g., used in strikes, wars, COVID). Hayek warned such powers erode liberty toward totalitarianism; without it, specific legislation per crisis would limit scope, force debate, and protect individualism and markets.

items.title: Places of Worship (Enfranchisement) Act 1920
items.summary: The Places of Worship (Enfranchisement) Act 1920 grants trustees holding leasehold interests in places of worship (churches, chapels, etc., including attached buildings) the statutory right to compulsorily acquire the freehold reversion and intermediate interests, converting leasehold into freehold ownership, subject to limits (e.g., max 2 acres) and procedures adapted from Lands Clauses Acts, while preserving trusts and covenants.
items.reason: Deletion would leave religious trustees perpetually subject to leasehold terms, ground rents, and reversionary interests, distorting incentives for maintenance and expansion as they lack full ownership security; landlords often refuse voluntary sales to preserve income streams. This Act secures private property rights for vital community institutions, fostering liberty and long-term investment without it, achieving stable tenure that markets alone rarely provide due to holdout problems.

items.title: Unemployment (Relief Works) Act 1920
items.summary: The Unemployment (Relief Works) Act 1920 authorised government funding for public works schemes to provide employment relief to the unemployed following World War I.
items.reason: Such make-work programs distort labour markets by artificially propping up wages and employment, crowding out private sector jobs, fostering dependency on state handouts, and diverting scarce resources from productive uses. Unseen consequences include higher taxes or inflation to fund them, moral hazard discouraging entrepreneurship and skill adaptation, and prolongation of economic adjustment periods, as markets naturally reallocate labour more efficiently without intervention.

items.title: Shops (Early Closing) Act 1920
items.summary: The Shops (Early Closing) Act 1920 requires shops to close early (typically by 8pm on weekdays) to limit working hours for shop assistants, with provisions for local variations and exemptions.
items.reason: This regulation distorts market incentives by overriding voluntary contracts between shop owners, employees, and consumers; it reduces operating hours, limiting supply of retail services when demand is high, raising costs, compressing employment opportunities, and stifling entrepreneurship. Unseen consequences include fewer jobs (as shops hire less for shorter hours), higher prices from inefficiency, reduced consumer choice (no late shopping), and potential black markets, all harming prosperity and liberty without evidence that markets wouldn't deliver better worker conditions through competition.

items.title: Appropriation (No. 2) Act 1920
items.summary: The Appropriation (No. 2) Act 1920 authorises the appropriation of funds from the Consolidated Fund for specified government services in the financial year ending 31 March 1921.
items.reason: This century-old act is entirely obsolete, with its authorisations long expired and superseded by subsequent appropriation legislation. Keeping it burdens the statute book with historical clutter, imposing unseen costs such as increased complexity in legal research, higher compliance and archival expenses for businesses and professionals, and diluted focus on active laws—distorting incentives for efficient governance without delivering any benefits to liberty, property rights, or prosperity.

items.title: Treaties of Peace (Austria and Bulgaria) Act 1920
items.summary: The Treaties of Peace (Austria and Bulgaria) Act 1920 (c. 6) was enacted to give legal effect in the UK to the post-World War I peace treaties with Austria (Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye) and Bulgaria (Treaty of Neuilly-sur-Seine), addressing matters such as territorial changes, reparations, and diplomatic relations.
items.reason: This century-old legislation served a specific historical purpose tied to WWI settlements, with provisions long superseded by modern international law, EU relations (pre-Brexit), and bilateral agreements. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter confuses citizens and businesses, raises compliance risks in an over-regulated system, diverts judicial resources, and perpetuates government overreach without delivering prosperity or liberty benefits today—deletion would streamline the statute book without harming current UK interests.

items.title: Isle of Man (Customs) Act 1920
items.summary: The Isle of Man (Customs) Act 1920 facilitates a customs union between the UK and the Isle of Man by allowing the application of UK customs duties and procedures to the Isle of Man, enabling duty-free movement of goods internally while aligning external tariffs.
items.reason: This act perpetuates an institutional framework for tariffs and customs controls, distorting trade incentives, raising consumer prices, and stifling competition through protectionism. Unintended consequences include bureaucratic overhead, reduced supply of cheaper imports, and barriers to entrepreneurial entry; deletion would dismantle outdated barriers, fostering freer bilateral trade and prosperity without reliance on government harmonization, as private markets would naturally optimize cross-border exchanges.

items.title: Public Works Loans Act 1920
items.summary: The Public Works Loans Act 1920 establishes and empowers the Public Works Loan Board (PWLB) to provide loans from public funds (via the National Loans Fund) to local authorities, public bodies, and certain private entities for infrastructure projects, public works, and other capital expenditures, often at subsidized rates backed by the Treasury.
items.reason: This Act institutionalizes government intervention in capital allocation, enabling cheap loans that distort interest rates, crowd out private lending and investment, foster moral hazard (borrowers face artificially low costs, leading to overspending on low-return projects), and centralize decision-making away from markets toward political priorities. Unseen consequences include higher national debt servicing costs borne by taxpayers, reduced private sector infrastructure innovation, local authority dependency reducing fiscal discipline, and inefficient resource use as politicians select projects lacking market viability. Without it, funding would shift to direct taxation, user fees, or open-market bonds, aligning incentives with actual costs and benefits to promote prosperity.

items.title: Women and Young Persons (Employment in Lead Processes) Act 1920
items.summary: The Women and Young Persons (Employment in Lead Processes) Act 1920 restricts or prohibits the employment of women and young persons (typically under 18) in industrial processes involving lead smelting, refining, or handling, to prevent health risks from lead poisoning.
items.reason: Retaining this century-old legislation imposes unseen costs by limiting employment opportunities for women and youth in lead-related industries, distorting labor markets, reducing workforce flexibility, and hindering economic mobility; it likely conflicts with or duplicates modern general health and safety laws (e.g., Health and Safety at Work Act 1974), creating compliance burdens and regulatory bloat without evidence of superior outcomes over market-driven protections like worker choice, liability, and innovation in safer processes.

items.title: Married Women (Maintenance) Act 1920
items.summary: The Married Women's (Maintenance) Act 1920 enables married women to obtain court orders for maintenance payments from husbands who desert them or fail to provide reasonable maintenance, addressing historical imbalances in marital property rights.
items.reason: This 104-year-old Act represents outdated state intervention in private family contracts, distorting incentives for marriage and personal responsibility by enabling adversarial court battles that increase legal costs, encourage dependency, and infringe on individual property rights. Modern family laws (e.g., Matrimonial Causes Act 1973) have superseded it, rendering it redundant; keeping it perpetuates bureaucratic bloat, unseen deadweight losses from compliance and litigation, and unintended consequences like reduced family stability through coerced transfers rather than voluntary agreements or prenups, hindering wealth creation via eroded incentives for self-reliance.

items.title: Married Women's Property (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Married Women's Property (Scotland) Act 1920 grants married women in Scotland independent rights to own, acquire, and dispose of property separately from their husbands, abolishing prior common law restrictions like coverture.
items.reason: Deleting this would leave Scottish married women vulnerable to spousal claims on their property under prior common law, eroding core private property rights for half the population, distorting incentives for individual economic participation, and harming liberty and prosperity; it achieves secure property ownership spontaneously through clear institutions, without which women would face reduced wealth creation and autonomy.

items.title: Employment of Women, Young Persons, and Children Act 1920
items.summary: The Employment of Women, Young Persons, and Children Act 1920 consolidates and regulates employment conditions for women, young persons (aged 14-18), and children, including restrictions on working hours, prohibitions on night work, and safeguards against industrial exploitation in factories and workshops.
items.reason: Retaining this 1920s-era legislation perpetuates rigid labor market distortions, raising compliance costs for businesses, reducing employment opportunities for women and youth by limiting flexibility, and creating barriers to entry for small firms while favoring incumbents. Unseen consequences include higher youth unemployment, suppressed wage competition, and stifled skill development through entry-level work; market incentives, contracts, reputation, and parental oversight achieve better outcomes without mandates.

items.title: Criminal Injuries (Ireland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Criminal Injuries (Ireland) Act 1920 establishes a scheme for compensation from public funds for personal injuries caused by riots, unlawful assemblies, or criminal acts in Ireland (pre-partition context).
items.reason: This taxpayer-funded compensation scheme distorts incentives by shifting costs from perpetrators to the public, creating moral hazard (reduced personal responsibility for security), bureaucratic overhead, and opportunities for fraud; unseen consequences include higher taxes crowding out private investment, disincentivizing private insurance or restitution via courts, and perpetuating dependency on state intervention rather than liberty-driven solutions like individual property rights protection and voluntary charity. Largely obsolete post-Irish partition with modern schemes superseding it.

items.title: Government of Ireland Act 1920 (repealed 2.12.1999)
items.summary: The Government of Ireland Act 1920 established separate parliaments for Southern Ireland (128 members) and Northern Ireland (52 members), a Council of Ireland for coordination towards potential unification, devolved legislative powers (excluding reserved matters like defense, foreign affairs, trade, coinage), executive structures under the Lord Lieutenant, financial arrangements including reserved taxes and an Irish contribution to Imperial expenditure, separate court systems with a shared High Court of Appeal, and transitional provisions for officers, police, and universities.
items.reason: This Act created layers of government, bureaucracy (e.g., Joint Exchequer Board, Council of Ireland), and fiscal distortions (reserved taxes, subsidies, fixed contributions) that distorted incentives, fueled conflict rather than prosperity, and failed to deliver liberty or wealth—evident in partition violence, economic stagnation, and supersession by later events (Anglo-Irish Treaty 1921, Irish Free State, NI's Stormont suspension 1972). Unseen costs include legal clutter, perpetuating obsolete entanglements harming UK clarity and individualism; deletion imposes no harm as provisions are overridden by modern NI devolution (1998 Act), Irish independence, and UK sovereignty (s.75 preserved). British citizens (inc. NI) worse off? No—current institutions achieve local governance without this relic's rigidities.

items.title: Juvenile Courts (Metropolis) Act 1920
items.summary: Establishes juvenile courts specifically in the metropolitan area of London (the Metropolis) to handle cases involving juvenile offenders, separating them from adult courts.
items.reason: This 1920 Act is largely obsolete, superseded by national frameworks like the Children and Young Persons Act 1933 (which extended juvenile courts UK-wide) and modern youth justice laws (e.g., Crime and Disorder Act 1998, Youth Justice and Criminal Evidence Act 1999). Keeping it imposes unseen costs: duplicative bureaucracy, regional inconsistencies in justice administration distorting incentives for uniform enforcement, compliance overhead for courts, and perpetuation of outdated procedures that hinder efficient resource allocation without improving outcomes for youth or public safety.

items.title: Registrar General (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: Establishes the office of the Registrar General for Scotland, responsible for the civil registration of births, deaths, marriages, and other vital events.
items.reason: Deletion would eliminate centralized, reliable vital records essential for proving identity, lineage, and legal status, severely undermining property rights, inheritance, contracts, and individual liberty—core prerequisites for wealth creation and prosperity that private markets rely upon but cannot uniformly provide without such foundational institutions.

items.title: Army and Air Force (Annual) Act 1920
items.summary: The Army and Air Force (Annual) Act 1920 was an annual renewal act that continued the legal existence, pay, discipline, enlistment, and operations of the British Army and Royal Air Force for one year, stemming from constitutional traditions against perpetual standing armies.
items.reason: This legislation institutionalizes a government monopoly on defense, funded by coercive taxation that crowds out private investment and distorts incentives for wealth creation. Unseen costs include military overspending, debt accumulation, inflation, foreign interventions that provoke conflicts rather than prosperity, and reduced individual liberty through conscription risks and state power concentration. Politicians' security rhetoric masks these failures, as history shows standing armies rarely enhance citizen welfare without better private alternatives like voluntary militias or market-based defense.

items.title: Gold and Silver (Export Control, &c.) Act 1920
items.summary: The Gold and Silver (Export Control, &c.) Act 1920 imposes controls on the export of gold and silver, restricting trade in these precious metals.
items.reason: Export controls violate property rights and free trade principles, distorting incentives, creating bureaucratic enforcement costs, enabling black markets, and reducing market liquidity without achieving sustainable economic benefits in a fiat currency era post-gold standard; unseen costs include stifled international exchange and diminished UK competitiveness.

items.title: Housing (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: The Housing (Scotland) Act 1920 empowered Scottish local authorities to build subsidised housing for the working classes to address post-WWI shortages, establishing government intervention in housing provision.
items.reason: This act exemplifies government distortion of housing markets through subsidies and public provision, crowding out private investment, inflating costs via taxpayer funding, creating dependency on state housing (e.g., long council waiting lists, poor maintenance), and unintended monopolies that stifle innovation and supply responsiveness; unseen consequences include reduced private construction incentives, misallocation of resources, and perpetuation of institutional failures in social housing that hinder prosperity and property rights.

items.title: Roads Act 1920 (repealed)
items.summary: The Roads Act 1920 establishes a system for county councils to levy excise duties on mechanically-propelled vehicles and carriages (replacing central collection), creates the Road Fund financed by these duties (after local allocations) for road improvements, introduces mandatory vehicle registration, licensing, identification marks (number plates), penalties for non-compliance, special provisions for dealers and hackney carriages, and amends prior road/vehicle laws to centralize administration under the Minister of Transport.
items.reason: This act imposes coercive excise taxes on vehicle ownership and use based on arbitrary metrics like weight, horsepower, and type, distorting incentives, raising transport costs, and reducing economic mobility, trade, and prosperity—especially harming the poor by limiting access to affordable vehicles. It creates bureaucratic layers (county councils, Minister oversight, Road Fund) with high administrative costs, evasion risks, and unintended monopolistic control over roads, diverting resources from private investment. The 'hypothecated' Road Fund proved illusory (later raided for general spending), failing to ensure efficient outcomes while enabling political capture. Unseen consequences include stifled automotive innovation, supply shortages in goods transport, and dependency on state infrastructure without user choice or competition; private tolls or general minimal taxation would better align incentives without such distortions.

items.title: Expiring Laws Continuance Act 1920
items.summary: The Expiring Laws Continuance Act 1920 extends the operation of certain temporary 'expiring laws', primarily wartime or emergency measures from the World War I era that were due to lapse.
items.reason: This 104-year-old act is obsolete; the temporary laws it prolonged have long expired, been repealed, or rendered irrelevant by subsequent legislation. Keeping it on the books creates unseen costs: legal clutter increases compliance burdens for businesses and individuals, fosters uncertainty in interpreting modern statutes, diverts administrative resources, and exemplifies regulatory creep where outdated provisions distort incentives without achieving any identifiable benefits to prosperity, liberty, or property rights.

items.title: British Empire Exhibition (Guarantee) Act 1920
items.summary: The British Empire Exhibition (Guarantee) Act 1920 provided a government financial guarantee to support the British Empire Exhibition, a promotional event held in the 1920s.
items.reason: This 1920 Act guaranteed taxpayer funds for a specific historical exhibition that concluded over a century ago, creating moral hazard by subsidizing a private promotional venture and distorting market incentives. It has no ongoing relevance, imposes unseen administrative costs in maintaining obsolete statutes, and exemplifies government picking winners rather than allowing private enterprise to fund such events organically, reducing liberty and efficiency without delivering sustained prosperity.

items.title: Official Secrets Act 1920 (repealed)
items.summary: The Official Secrets Act 1920 amends the 1911 Act to criminalize unauthorized use of uniforms/documents for accessing prohibited places, retention/communication of official secrets, dealings with foreign agents, interference with security personnel, mandatory production of telegrams, registration/regulation of postal forwarding businesses, duty to report suspected offenses, and related attempts/incitements, with expanded definitions and penalties for national security protection.
items.reason: Deleting this legislation would remove critical institutional protections against espionage, sabotage, and foreign interference, leaving UK defense secrets, munitions, and prohibited places vulnerable to exploitation. British citizens would be worse off without these deterrents, as common law fraud/impersonation inadequately addresses state-specific threats like code word leaks or agent communications, potentially enabling enemy intelligence gathering that weakens military readiness, invites aggression, and erodes national sovereignty—foundational to liberty, property rights, and prosperity. Minor regulatory costs (e.g., postal registration, reporting duties) pale against these existential risks, with no evidence of systemic abuse outweighing security gains in achieving deterrence without reliance on decree alone.

items.title: Agriculture Act 1920
items.summary: The Agriculture Act 1920 established the Agricultural Wages Board to fix minimum wages for agricultural workers in England and Wales, provided for arbitration of wage disputes, and included provisions for smallholdings, allotments, and agricultural development schemes.
items.reason: Minimum wage boards distort labor markets by overriding voluntary contracts between employers and workers, leading to unemployment (especially for low-skilled rural workers), reduced hiring, higher food prices, and accelerated mechanization that displaces labor. These interventions create dependency on government pricing rather than market signals, stifle agricultural competitiveness, and impose unseen costs like rural depopulation and suppressed entrepreneurship, outweighing any short-term wage gains as markets naturally balance supply/demand for fair outcomes.

items.title: Dyestuffs (Import Regulations) Act 1920
items.summary: The Dyestuffs (Import Regulations) Act 1920 imposes controls on the importation of dyestuffs (chemical dyes) via licensing or quotas to protect Britain's post-WWI domestic industry from foreign competition, particularly Germany.
items.reason: This protectionist measure distorts markets by restricting imports, raising dyestuff prices for UK textile, chemical, and manufacturing industries, reducing supply, stifling competition, and encouraging inefficiency in protected producers. Unseen costs include higher consumer prices, lost jobs in downstream sectors, reduced innovation, and barriers to comparative advantage; free trade would lower costs and boost prosperity without government decree.

items.title: Juries (Emergency Provisions) Act 1920
items.summary: The Juries (Emergency Provisions) Act 1920 empowers authorities to make special jury selection arrangements during emergencies, such as strikes, to allow trials to proceed by drawing jurors from outside affected areas.
items.reason: Retaining this century-old emergency measure imposes unseen costs by granting vague discretionary powers that could be abused to stack juries in favor of the state during politically motivated 'emergencies', undermining the impartiality of trial by jury—a cornerstone of liberty. It distorts incentives for fair justice, creates moral hazard for future overreach, and clutters the legal framework; true crises can be handled ad hoc without permanent provisions that invite unintended biases and reduce trust in institutions.

items.title: Defence of the Realm (Acquisition of Land) Act 1920
items.summary: Amends the 1916 Defence of the Realm (Acquisition of Land) Act to facilitate government disposal of wartime-acquired land free from restrictive covenants (with compensation capped at £50,000 total), modifies pre-emption rights, clarifies acquisition/possession powers including transfers between departments, handles easements and railway/public land, and temporarily continues military range byelaws until 1922.
items.reason: This post-WWI legislation entrenches government overreach by overriding private restrictive covenants on compulsorily acquired land during disposal, distorting property markets and incentives for development; taxpayer-funded compensation (capped) creates moral hazard and unseen fiscal burdens; clarifies expansive possession/transfer powers that prolong state control beyond war needs; overall, it undermines private property rights and institutions essential for wealth creation, with most provisions now obsolete yet cluttering the statute book and risking future abuse.

items.title: House-Letting and Rating (Scotland) Act 1920
items.summary: Amends Scottish law on liability for local rates (property taxes) for rented dwelling-houses, particularly making owners liable for rates on unfurnished lets in tenements instead of tenants.
items.reason: Mandates shifting tax liability from tenants to landlords, overriding free contracts between private parties and distorting incentives: landlords face higher costs (likely passed to tenants via rents), reducing supply of rental housing, discouraging property investment, and creating administrative burdens with unseen consequences like tenement decay and reduced market efficiency in Scotland's housing sector, all contrary to liberty and private property.

items.title: Air Navigation Act 1920
items.summary: The Air Navigation Act 1920 establishes the foundational framework for regulating civil air navigation in the UK, including aircraft registration, pilot and aircraft licensing, airworthiness standards, and prohibitions on unauthorized flight.
items.reason: British citizens would be significantly worse off without this legislation, as it provides essential institutions for managing shared airspace—a public good vulnerable to tragedy of the commons. Absent standardized safety rules, pilot qualifications, and airworthiness requirements, aviation accidents would surge, eroding trust, spiking insurance costs, deterring investment, and crippling air travel critical for trade, tourism, and prosperity. Markets cannot self-regulate externalities like mid-air collisions or unsafe aircraft without such foundational rules, and the Act demonstrably enables safe, high-volume aviation that drives economic growth, outweighing regulatory costs through preserved lives and liberty of movement.

items.title: Administration of Justice Act 1920
items.summary: The Administration of Justice Act 1920 amends procedural aspects of the UK justice system, including expanding trial powers for matrimonial causes, providing flexibility for trials without juries in certain cases, streamlining grand jury proceedings, extending Admiralty jurisdiction, enabling reciprocal enforcement of civil judgments across His Majesty's dominions, allowing ex-judges to sit temporarily, regulating arbitration appointments, permitting corporations to receive probate, standardising fees and bonds, and other judicial efficiencies, primarily for England and Wales.
items.reason: This Act enhances the efficiency, flexibility, and reciprocity of the judicial system, which is vital for enforcing contracts, protecting property rights, and facilitating trade—core institutions for wealth creation and liberty. Provisions like reciprocal judgment enforcement reduce cross-jurisdictional barriers, promoting commerce within the Empire; jury trial flexibility cuts costs and delays without undermining rights; Admiralty expansions and arbitration rules lower dispute resolution expenses. Deleting it would revert to rigid, outdated procedures, causing delays, higher costs, and weakened enforcement, leaving citizens worse off with slower justice, deterred investment, and distorted incentives for economic activity, as reliable courts are indispensable and would not spontaneously recreate these efficiencies.

items.title: Unemployment Insurance (Temporary Provisions Amendment) Act 1920
items.summary: The Unemployment Insurance (Temporary Provisions Amendment) Act 1920 amends temporary provisions in the Unemployment Insurance Act 1920, likely extending or modifying short-term government-mandated unemployment benefits in response to post-World War I economic conditions.
items.reason: Government-mandated unemployment insurance distorts labor market incentives by subsidizing non-work, creating moral hazard that prolongs unemployment and reduces workforce participation; it imposes compulsory contributions (taxes in disguise) that raise business costs, discourage hiring, and divert capital from productive private investment. Unseen consequences include entrenched dependency, bureaucratic inefficiencies far worse than voluntary mutual aid or private charity, stifled wage flexibility, and long-term structural unemployment, all undermining wealth creation through liberty and property rights. Even as a 'temporary' measure, retaining such interventions perpetuates harmful institutions without delivering promised poverty alleviation.

items.title: Public Utility Companies (Capital Issues) Act 1920
items.summary: The Public Utility Companies (Capital Issues) Act 1920 regulated the issuance of shares, stock, and loans by public utility companies (e.g., gas, water, electricity, transport), requiring Treasury consent to prevent over-capitalisation and protect consumers from inflated charges.
items.reason: This act exemplifies government overreach into private capital markets, distorting incentives for investment and innovation in essential services. Unintended consequences include bureaucratic delays raising costs, stifling new entrants and competition, enabling regulatory capture by incumbents, and reducing overall supply and efficiency in utilities—hurting consumers more than helping, as markets and private property rights better allocate capital without central planning failures noted by Hayek and Mises.

items.title: Unemployment Insurance Act 1921
items.summary: The Unemployment Insurance Act 1921 extended compulsory unemployment insurance to most UK workers, mandating contributions from employees and employers into a state fund to provide benefits during unemployment periods.
items.reason: Compulsory state insurance distorts labor markets by introducing moral hazard—unemployed workers have less incentive to seek jobs quickly, employers face higher hiring costs reducing employment, and contributions act as a tax stifling wage competition and mobility. Unseen consequences include elevated structural unemployment, dependency culture eroding self-reliance, bureaucratic overhead diverting funds from genuine aid, and crowding out superior private alternatives like mutual societies or voluntary insurance, which historically provided aid without coercion and better aligned incentives for wealth creation.

items.title: Mr. Speaker's Retirement Act 1921
items.summary: The Mr. Speaker's Retirement Act 1921 (c. 10) is a historical, one-off UK Public General Act providing for the retirement arrangements of the Speaker of the House of Commons in 1921.
items.reason: This obsolete, ad-hoc legislation addresses a single individual's retirement from over a century ago, with no ongoing regulatory effect, public benefit, or institutional necessity.
