Keeping it on the books incurs unseen costs: cluttering the statute book, complicating legal research, symbolizing government favoritism through special laws rather than general rules, and diverting focus from timeless institutions to historical trivia that distorts incentives for efficient governance.

items.title: Treaty of Peace (Hungary) Act 1921
items.summary: The Treaty of Peace (Hungary) Act 1921 (c. 11) is a UK Public General Act that gives legal effect in the UK to the Treaty of Trianon, the post-WWI peace treaty signed between the Allied Powers and Hungary on 4 June 1920.
items.reason: This 1921 Act fulfilled its narrow, one-time purpose of domesticating a century-old treaty; it imposes no ongoing obligations, regulations, or burdens on private property, trade, or liberty today. Retaining it clogs the statute book with obsolete verbiage, risks interpretive confusion in modern legal contexts (e.g., archaic territorial or reparations clauses irrelevant post-EU/NATO integration), distorts focus from living institutions to historical artifacts, and incurs unseen compliance/administrative costs for lawyers, courts, and citizens navigating redundant laws—better deleted to streamline the legal system and affirm that institutions evolve without perpetual state encumbrance.

items.title: Public Health (Tuberculosis) Act 1921
items.summary: The Public Health (Tuberculosis) Act 1921 empowers local sanitary authorities to establish and maintain institutions for the diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of tuberculosis, including sanatoria, dispensaries, and residential accommodation, funded by local rates, and provides for notifications and related public health measures.
items.reason: This century-old Act is largely obsolete, superseded by the NHS and modern public health laws like the Health and Social Care Act 2012 and Public Health England frameworks, which handle TB control more efficiently. It distorts healthcare markets by mandating and funding government-specific TB institutions, crowding out private provision, innovation, and competition; raises local taxes diverting resources from productive uses; and perpetuates bureaucratic overhead with unseen costs like reduced incentives for medical advancements. TB incidence has plummeted due to rising prosperity, improved living standards, nutrition, sanitation, and antibiotics—not state decrees—proving markets and liberty achieve better outcomes without such interventions.

items.title: Captive Birds Shooting (Prohibition) Act 1921
items.summary: Prohibits the shooting of captive birds, targeting cruel practices like releasing confined birds in enclosures for target shooting.
items.reason: This 1921 law exemplifies paternalistic overreach into private property and individual liberty, banning a niche activity long obsolete due to evolving social norms and voluntary animal welfare standards. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: statutory clutter that burdens legal navigation for businesses and citizens; potential chilling of innovative uses like humane training simulations; distorted incentives where government moralizing supplants market-driven reputation and boycotts; and reinforces institutional bias toward prohibition over spontaneous order, yielding no measurable prosperity gain while eroding individualism.

items.title: Protection of Animals Act (1911) Amendment Act 1921
items.summary: This 1921 Act amends the Protection of Animals Act 1911, section 1(3)(b), by adding a clause stating that coursing or hunting a captive animal in an enclosed space from which it has no reasonable chance of escape is not deemed 'coursed or hunted' under the subsection. This closes a loophole, ensuring such 'canned' activities do not qualify for the exemption from general animal cruelty prohibitions.
items.reason: Retaining this legislation imposes government overreach into private property rights over animals, distorting incentives for rural sports, pest control, and animal training by subjecting owner-controlled activities to arbitrary definitions of 'hunting.' Unseen costs include compliance burdens, legal uncertainties for property owners, and stifled market-driven animal use innovations; without it, common law and voluntary norms would suffice, enhancing liberty without state micromanagement.

items.title: Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Act 1921
items.summary: The Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Act 1921 extended compulsory unemployment insurance to additional trades and workers, mandating contributions from employees, employers, and the state to fund benefits for the unemployed.
items.reason: Compulsory state insurance distorts labor markets by creating moral hazard—reducing incentives to seek work quickly—increasing structural unemployment and dependency; contributions act as taxes that shrink wages, profits, and investment; bureaucratic administration wastes resources and stifles private, competitive insurance options that would innovate and price risks efficiently; unseen costs include eroded work ethic, higher long-term joblessness, and precedent for expansive welfare state crowding out voluntary mutual aid and charity.

items.title: Importation of Plumage (Prohibition) Act 1921
items.summary: Prohibits the importation of plumage (bird feathers) into the United Kingdom.
items.reason: This 1921 Act aimed to curb the trade in exotic bird feathers for fashion, which has largely ended due to changing tastes and international wildlife protections. Keeping it imposes ongoing trade barriers, enforcement costs, and market distortions, potentially blocking sustainable feather uses (e.g., fly fishing, crafts) while smugglers evade it anyway; unseen costs include reduced economic liberty and property rights without proven net benefits over voluntary markets or targeted modern regulations.

items.title: Agriculture (Amendment) Act 1921
items.summary: The Agriculture (Amendment) Act 1921 (c. 17) amends prior agricultural legislation, likely tweaking post-WWI measures such as subsidies, wage controls, or land regulations for farmers, though full text is PDF-only.
items.reason: Retaining century-old agricultural amendments perpetuates government distortions in farming markets, fostering dependency on state support, inefficient resource allocation, higher food prices for consumers, and reduced innovation as private incentives are crowded out by regulatory rigidities and unintended monopolistic tendencies in subsidized sectors—outcomes at odds with wealth creation via liberty and property rights.

items.title: Indian Divorces (Validity) Act 1921
items.summary: The Indian Divorces (Validity) Act 1921 validates divorces granted by courts in British India prior to a specified date for recognition in the UK.
items.reason: This colonial-era law addressed a narrow historical issue of recognising divorces from British India, now obsolete post-1947 independence and superseded by modern UK family law (e.g., Matrimonial Causes Act 1973, Family Law Act 1986). Retaining it creates statutory clutter, increases legal research costs, confuses practitioners, and distorts incentives by perpetuating irrelevant complexity without achieving any ongoing benefits for liberty or prosperity.

items.title: Housing Act 1921
items.summary: The Housing Act 1921 provided Exchequer grants and financial assistance to local authorities and private builders for constructing houses to address post-World War I shortages, enabling subsidized housing development.
items.reason: This act exemplifies government intervention via subsidies, which distorts housing market incentives by artificially boosting supply through taxpayer funds, crowding out private investment, creating bureaucratic overhead, and fostering dependency on state aid. Unintended consequences include misallocated resources (taxes diverted from productive uses), moral hazard (builders prioritizing subsidized projects over market needs), and long-term supply shortages as regulations stifle private development. Private property and liberty-driven markets would better match supply to demand, lowering costs and increasing quality without unseen fiscal burdens.

items.title: Consolidated Fund (No. 1) Act 1921
items.summary: The Consolidated Fund (No. 1) Act 1921 authorizes the issue of specific sums from the UK's Consolidated Fund to cover government expenditures for the financial year ending 31 March 1922.
items.reason: This 1921 Act is entirely obsolete, as the authorized expenditures occurred a century ago and have no ongoing effect. Retaining it clutters the statute book, fosters legal uncertainty by leaving defunct provisions on record, and imposes unseen administrative costs through maintenance, research, and potential misinterpretation in legal contexts. Deletion would streamline the legislative corpus, reduce compliance burdens, and align with institutional clarity without impacting fiscal operations, which are handled by contemporary appropriation acts.

items.title: Tithe Annuities Apportionment Act 1921 (repealed)
items.summary: An Act to facilitate the apportionment of tithe redemption annuities charged on land under the Tithe Act 1918, allowing ministerial orders for division among land parcels, mandatory redemption of small annuities, registration as land charges, and use of settled land capital for redemption.
items.reason: This 1921 legislation addresses a narrow, historical issue arising from tithe rentcharge redemptions, which were largely completed by subsequent acts like the Tithe Act 1936. It imposes government administrative processes (ministerial orders, fees, registrations) on private land transactions, creating bureaucratic hurdles and potential costs even if rarely invoked today. Unseen consequences include distorting incentives for land division/sale by tying encumbrances to outdated state machinery rather than market-driven resolutions or common law; deleting it simplifies the statute book, reduces regulatory clutter, and empowers private property rights without evident harm, as no significant tithe annuities remain.

items.title: Dentists Act 1921
items.summary: The Dentists Act 1921 regulates the dental profession by creating a General Dental Council (successor to the Dental Board), establishing a register of qualified dentists, setting qualification standards, and prohibiting unlicensed practice of dentistry.
items.reason: Occupational licensing like this creates barriers to entry, restricting supply of dentists, inflating costs, and exacerbating shortages (e.g., NHS dental access crises), while stifling innovation in roles like dental therapists. Unseen costs include reduced competition, higher prices harming the poor most, distorted incentives for over-regulation by incumbents, and market exclusion of low-cost providers; private certification, reputation, malpractice insurance, and consumer choice would better ensure quality and access without state monopoly.

items.title: Protection of Animals (Scotland) Act, 1912, Amendment Act 1921 (repealed)
items.summary: Amends the Protection of Animals (Scotland) Act 1912 by exempting the coursing or hunting of captive animals in enclosed spaces from which they have no reasonable chance of escape from being deemed prohibited 'coursing or hunting'.
items.reason: Deleting this amendment would reinstate a broader prohibition, curtailing property owners' rights to engage in controlled recreational activities like enclosed coursing on private land, distorting incentives for traditional sports, potentially driving them underground, and imposing unnecessary government restrictions on individual liberty without clear evidence of net benefits outweighing costs; citizens would be worse off with reduced freedoms and innovation in low-harm animal uses.

items.title: Public Health (Officers) Act 1921
items.summary: Provides for the superannuation (pensions) of officers appointed under the Public Health Acts, such as medical officers of health and sanitary inspectors, allowing local authorities to grant retirement allowances funded by ratepayers.
items.reason: Establishes taxpayer-funded pensions for public health bureaucrats, creating lifelong entitlements that distort labor markets by favoring public sector employment over private enterprise. Unseen costs include higher local taxes or rates, fiscal drag on private investment and wealth creation, reduced incentives for efficiency in public health services, and entrenchment of government monopoly over sanitation and health inspection, preventing competitive private alternatives that could innovate and lower costs.

items.title: Deceased Brother's Widow's Marriage Act 1921
items.summary: The Deceased Brother's Widow's Marriage Act 1921 legalized marriage between a man and his deceased brother's widow, which was previously prohibited under ecclesiastical law.
items.reason: This act was a limited liberalization of marriage restrictions in 1921, but it has been superseded and repealed by subsequent legislation, including the Marriage Act 1949 and the Marriage (Prohibited Degrees of Relationship) Act 1986, which broadened permissible relationships. Retaining this obsolete law contributes to legislative bloat, increasing unseen costs in legal research, interpretation, and maintenance without providing any ongoing benefit to liberty or prosperity, as modern laws already permit such marriages.

items.title: National Health Insurance Act 1921
items.summary: The National Health Insurance Act 1921 expanded compulsory national health insurance, providing sickness, maternity, and medical benefits to employed persons and dependents, funded by worker, employer, and state contributions.
items.reason: This act mandates government-run insurance, distorting private health markets by crowding out voluntary alternatives, creating moral hazard (reduced personal responsibility for health), bureaucratic inefficiencies, and path dependency toward full socialization like the NHS. Unseen costs include suppressed innovation, higher long-term expenses from distorted incentives, reduced labor mobility, and wealth transfer via coercion rather than markets; deletion restores individual choice, competition, and property rights in healthcare, fostering prosperity without these interventions.

items.title: Overseas Trade (Credits and Insurance) Amendment Act 1921
items.summary: An amendment to prior legislation enabling government provision of credits and insurance for overseas trade, facilitating state-backed export financing and risk guarantees.
items.reason: This act institutionalizes government intervention in private trade finance, distorting market signals by subsidizing exporters with taxpayer-backed insurance and credits. Unseen costs include moral hazard (exporters taking undue risks), crowding out private insurers and banks, political allocation of guarantees favoring connected firms over efficient ones, capital misallocation toward less competitive exports, and potential fiscal burdens from defaults—preventing the natural evolution of robust private markets that would better price risks and spur innovation.

items.title: Health Resorts and Watering Places Act 1921
items.summary: The Health Resorts and Watering Places Act 1921 empowers local authorities in designated health resorts and seaside towns (watering places) to adopt special bylaws for providing and regulating public amenities like seating, conveniences, bandstands, and promenades to attract tourists.
items.reason: Retaining this 1921 Act perpetuates government intervention in tourism amenities, crowding out private enterprise that could more efficiently supply demanded facilities through market competition. Tax-funded public provisions distort incentives, raise local rates (taxes), foster bureaucracy, and lead to unintended consequences like poor maintenance (tragedy of the commons), reduced innovation, and regulatory barriers to private property use. Modern local government powers likely overlap, making it redundant; deletion would unleash entrepreneurial liberty to boost seaside prosperity without coercive institutions.

items.title: Merchant Shipping Act 1921
items.summary: Amends Merchant Shipping Acts 1894-1920 to extend ship registry, liability limitation, and safety rules to lighters, barges, and similar vessels used in navigation in Great Britain, with provisions for equivalent local port regulations and penalties for using unsafe vessels.
items.reason: This legislation standardizes registration and tonnage measurement for smaller commercial vessels critical to port and coastal trade, reducing transaction costs and enabling liability limitation (Part VIII) that encourages investment in shipping by capping owner risk—without it, operators might avoid these vessels due to unlimited liability, shrinking supply and raising transport costs, harming prosperity. Safety prohibition on dangerously unsafe vessels (defective, overloaded, undermanned) addresses externalities in shared navigable waters where recklessness endangers others, with Board of Trade oversight providing minimal enforcement; deletion would likely increase accidents and blockages without market alternatives fully emerging quickly. Flexibility for local regs and worker liability preservation limit burdens.

items.title: Church of Scotland Act 1921
items.summary: The Church of Scotland Act 1921 declares lawful the Declaratory Articles affirming the Church of Scotland's spiritual constitution, independence from civil authority in doctrine, worship, government, and discipline, while preserving civil courts' jurisdiction over civil matters and not prejudicing other churches.
items.reason: Deleting this Act would reintroduce legal doubts about the Church's spiritual autonomy, potentially inviting state interference in religious doctrine and governance, eroding individual religious liberty and the institutional separation of church and state. This liberty fosters voluntary association and community without coercive government distortion, aligning with principles of individualism and private voluntary institutions; without the Act, prior inconsistent statutes could prevail, harming Scots' freedom and prosperity through reduced spiritual and associational choices that Hayek and Friedman would view as essential to a free society.

items.title: Consolidated Fund (No. 2) Act 1921
items.summary: The Consolidated Fund (No. 2) Act 1921 is an appropriation act authorizing the issue of specific sums from the UK's Consolidated Fund for government expenditures in the fiscal year ending 31 March 1922.
items.reason: This is an obsolete 1921 spending authorization for a long-concluded fiscal period. The funds were issued over a century ago, and the Act has no ongoing effect. Retaining it clutters the statute book, imposes unseen administrative costs in legal maintenance and reference, and exemplifies how historical fiscal measures persist without purpose, distorting focus from current institutions toward irrelevant relics rather than streamlining for liberty and efficiency.

items.title: Coroners Act 1921
items.summary: The Coroners Act 1921 regulates the office of coroner in England and Wales, establishing procedures for inquests into unnatural, sudden, or suspicious deaths, including those in custody, to determine cause and circumstances, while setting qualifications and duties for coroners.
items.reason: Deletion would leave no impartial, standardized state mechanism for investigating suspicious deaths, risking cover-ups by powerful interests, negligence in custody, or unaccountable accidents; private alternatives would lack universality, impartiality, and incentives for the poor/powerless, eroding rule of law, public trust, and deterrence of harm—core to protecting life/liberty without which prosperity falters as Hayek noted institutions underpin order; costs are bureaucratic but unseen benefits in justice/prevention outweigh, unlike distortive regs.

items.title: Police Pensions Act 1921
items.summary: The Police Pensions Act 1921 established a contributory pension scheme for members of police forces in the UK, providing retirement benefits funded by contributions and government support.
items.reason: Maintaining this act imposes ongoing fiscal burdens on taxpayers through unfunded liabilities and rigid public sector pension obligations, distorting labor markets by incentivizing early retirement, overstaffing, and reduced productivity in policing. It crowds out private savings and investment, perpetuates government monopoly on retirement provision for public employees, and creates moral hazard where officers face less personal risk in financial planning. Unseen consequences include stifled incentives for innovation in private pension markets and higher taxes that reduce overall wealth creation and individual liberty; market-driven compensation would better align recruitment, retention, and efficiency without state intervention.

items.title: Finance Act 1921
items.summary: The Finance Act 1921 continues, imposes, amends, and repeals various customs, excise, income tax (at 6 shillings), super-tax, entertainment, and other duties; terminates excess profits duty after final periods; provides exemptions (e.g., charities, superannuation funds), adjustments for stock valuations, death duties relief, national debt sinking funds, and miscellaneous financial provisions.
items.reason: This act exemplifies interventionist fiscal policy through protective tariffs (customs/excise duties on tea, wine, medicines, etc.), high income/super-tax rates, and administrative complexities that distort incentives, raise costs, reduce trade/supply, and infringe private property rights. Even positive elements like terminating excess profits duty are offset by legacy burdens, stock relief gimmicks, and perpetuation of tax-funded debt schemes. Unseen costs include stifled entrepreneurship, capital flight (rich leaving), misallocated resources (e.g., favors to charities/utilities), bureaucratic overhead, and long-term erosion of productivity/liberty, preventing spontaneous market order and wealth creation via free exchange.

items.title: Housing (Scotland) Act 1921
items.summary: The Housing (Scotland) Act 1921 empowered Scottish local authorities to construct subsidised housing for the working classes, providing government financial assistance to address post-WWI shortages.
items.reason: This act exemplifies government intervention distorting housing markets: subsidies crowd out private builders, inflate costs via malinvestment (as per Austrian business cycle theory), foster dependency and bureaucracy, reduce supply through regulatory capture, and fail to sustainably improve affordability—evidenced by ongoing UK housing crises despite decades of such policies. Unseen costs include stifled innovation, capital misallocation from productive uses, and weakened property rights signals, harming long-term prosperity more than any short-term relief.

items.title: Representation of the People Act 1921
items.summary: The Representation of the People Act 1921 removed the legal bar preventing women from standing for election to Parliament, enabling their full participation in the democratic process.
items.reason: Deleting this legislation would reimpose a severe restriction on individual liberty, barring half the population from seeking political office and risking entrenchment of unrepresentative institutions that undermine rule of law, accountability, and the checks necessary for protecting economic freedoms and private property—outcomes that spontaneous order and market prosperity depend on, as emphasized by Hayek. Without this Act, equal political participation would not occur, leaving citizens (especially women) demonstrably worse off in a less liberal system prone to capture by special interests.

items.title: Corn Sales Act 1921
items.summary: The Corn Sales Act 1921 mandates that all contracts for corn (wheat, barley, oats, rye, maize, peas, beans, potatoes, seeds, etc.) must be by weight using the hundredweight of 112 imperial pounds, rendering non-compliant contracts null and void (with exceptions for small quantities, imports/exports, and unharvested corn). It amends the Corn Returns Act 1882 and Corn Production Acts 1917/1920 to use weight instead of bushels for returns, averages, and minimum prices, and adapts old payment provisions to equivalent weights.
items.reason: This act violates freedom of contract by nullifying voluntary agreements not using a government-mandated unit, imposing compliance costs (e.g., scales, training) on farmers and traders, distorting incentives in agriculture, and propping up interventionist price controls from prior acts. Unseen consequences include reduced market entry for small sellers, stifled innovation in measurement standards, higher food prices from supply constraints, and perpetuation of bureaucratic oversight tying into failed subsidy regimes that fail to aid the poor while driving away efficient producers.

items.title: Juries (Emergency Provisions) (Renewal) Act 1921
items.summary: The Juries (Emergency Provisions) (Renewal) Act 1921 renews temporary WWI-era emergency measures altering jury procedures, likely reducing jury sizes or modifying trial processes to address wartime shortages.
items.reason: This 1921 renewal of century-old emergency provisions is obsolete, cluttering the statute book and imposing unseen costs: legal research burdens on courts/lawyers, maintenance expenses in databases, uncertainty over dormant powers that could be revived abusively, and distortion of incentives by perpetuating a bloated, unreadable legal corpus that erodes rule of law clarity—core to liberty and efficient justice—without delivering benefits in peacetime.

items.title: Territorial Army and Militia Act 1921
items.summary: The Territorial Army and Militia Act 1921 established and regulated the Territorial Army (volunteer reserve force) and Militia, covering organization, training, pay, and administration following World War I.
items.reason: This 1921 Act has been substantially repealed and superseded by modern legislation including the Reserve Forces Act 1980, Reserve Forces Act 1996, and Armed Forces Act 2006, which govern today's Army Reserve. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: legal clutter confuses practitioners and citizens, raises compliance and interpretation expenses for any residual clauses, distorts incentives by preserving obsolete references, and hinders statute book simplification without delivering defense benefits, as effective institutions have evolved organically via later laws. Deletion promotes clarity, reduces bureaucratic drag, and aligns with prosperity through streamlined governance.

items.title: Salmon and Freshwater Fisheries Act 1921
items.summary: The Salmon and Freshwater Fisheries Act 1921 consolidates and regulates salmon and freshwater fisheries in England and Wales, establishing licensing requirements, close seasons, prohibitions on certain fishing methods, protections for fish stocks, and fishery boards with enforcement powers.
items.reason: This act imposes regulatory burdens on private property owners with riparian rights and anglers, creating bureaucracy via government-appointed fishery boards that distort incentives, raise compliance costs, and enable regulatory capture. Unintended consequences include reduced fishing participation, barriers to entry for small operators, inefficient resource allocation, and stifled innovation in sustainable practices. Private property rights, voluntary cooperatives, or market-based quotas would better prevent overfishing tragedies of the commons without coercive state intervention, fostering prosperity in the sector.

items.title: Admiralty Pensions Act 1921
items.summary: The Admiralty Pensions Act 1921 allows commutation of naval pensions into lump sums, restoration of forfeited pensions with provisions for dependents during imprisonment, adjusts Coast Guard service reckoning for pensions, and applies to certain war injury compensations.
items.reason: This Act fulfills specific, historical obligations to naval personnel and war veterans by enabling flexible pension administration (commutation, restoration, dependent support), which would not occur without it. Deleting it would leave these citizens—many who served in WWI—worse off by denying promised benefits, eroding trust in state commitments akin to contract repudiation, with negligible ongoing costs or market distortions given its narrow, administrative scope.

items.title: Children Act 1921
items.summary: The Children Act 1921 enabled courts to commit neglected or at-risk children (including illegitimate children) to the care of local authorities, expanding state intervention in family matters for child protection and welfare.
items.reason: Retaining this legislation entrenches government monopoly over child welfare, distorting family incentives by encouraging state dependency over private responsibility, spawning bureaucratic foster systems with high failure rates, abuse scandals, and massive unseen costs that crowd out effective charities, communities, and family networks proven superior pre-intervention; liberty and property rights in child-rearing are eroded without proportional benefits unattainable via common law protections against abuse or voluntary aid.

items.title: Isle of Man (Customs) Act 1921
items.summary: The Isle of Man (Customs) Act 1921 makes provision for the application of UK customs enactments to the Isle of Man, enabling alignment of customs duties and procedures between the UK and the Isle of Man as a Crown Dependency.
items.reason: This act perpetuates customs controls and potential tariff alignments, distorting trade incentives, imposing bureaucratic costs, and raising prices for goods moving between the UK and Isle of Man or externally. Unseen consequences include reduced trade volumes, sheltered inefficiencies in local industries, and barriers to consumer choice, all contrary to free market prosperity. Deletion would foster unrestricted trade, enhancing liberty, competition, and wealth creation without reliance on government-imposed duties.

items.title: Greenwich Hospital Act 1921
items.summary: The Greenwich Hospital Act 1921 provides for the management, properties, revenues, and administration of Greenwich Hospital (originally for aged seamen, now supporting Royal Navy personnel via a self-funding charitable trust), including appointment of commissioners and governance rules.
items.reason: While self-funding via dedicated properties rather than general taxation, this act entrenches government-appointed oversight and rigid statutory structures for a specific institution, distorting incentives for efficient property management and allocation compared to fully private charities under general law. Unseen costs include path-dependent bureaucracy, reduced adaptability to market changes, and foregone opportunities for competitive innovation in naval welfare support; deletion allows operation under modern charity frameworks, enhancing liberty and prosperity without harming beneficiaries.

items.title: Licensing Act 1921
items.summary: The Licensing Act 1921 consolidates and amends prior laws on intoxicating liquor licensing, regulating pub/club hours, license grants/transfers, sales to minors, and registration of clubs for alcohol supply.
items.reason: This act imposes government-controlled barriers to entry via scarce licenses, creating cartel-like rents for incumbents, bureaucratic costs, and reduced supply of venues; unintended consequences include higher consumer prices, fewer hospitality jobs/innovation, distorted incentives (e.g., closing-time rushes), and failure to curb harms (as seen historically with prohibitionism) while limiting private property rights and individual choice—markets would self-regulate via reputation, contracts, and tort liability, fostering prosperity without state distortion.

items.title: Land Settlement Amendment Act 1921
items.summary: Amends the Land Settlement (Facilities) Act 1919 to extend provisions for local authorities to acquire land and create smallholdings, primarily for ex-servicemen after World War I.
items.reason: Government-mandated land redistribution distorts private property rights and market allocation of land, creating inefficient smallholdings reliant on subsidies; unseen costs include bureaucratic overhead, reduced agricultural productivity from mismatched incentives, and precedent for ongoing state intervention that crowds out voluntary market transactions and entrepreneurship.

items.title: Water Undertakings (Modification of Charges) Act 1921
items.summary: The Water Undertakings (Modification of Charges) Act 1921 allows for government modification of charges imposed by water supply undertakings.
items.reason: This legislation enables price controls on water services, distorting market incentives by preventing undertakings from setting prices based on costs, demand, and competition. Unseen consequences include reduced investment in infrastructure maintenance and expansion, leading to shortages, deteriorating service quality, and higher long-term taxpayer costs via subsidies; it entrenches monopolies by discouraging new entrants and innovation, as firms cannot recover costs efficiently, harming prosperity and liberty more than any short-term consumer protection.

items.title: Duchy of Lancaster (Application of Capital Moneys) Act 1921
items.summary: The Duchy of Lancaster (Application of Capital Moneys) Act 1921 permits the application of capital moneys from the Duchy of Lancaster estate (a hereditary private estate providing income to the monarch) for specific purposes, likely maintenance, repairs, or improvements that were previously restricted under trust rules.
items.reason: This obscure 1921 act grants special parliamentary permission for managing a single quasi-private estate's capital, perpetuating unequal treatment under the law and feudal privileges. General modern property, trust, and investment laws suffice for efficient management, rendering this unnecessary. Keeping it bloats the statute book, distorts incentives by implying special rules are needed over universal ones, and imposes unseen compliance costs on Duchy administrators without broad benefits to liberty or prosperity. Deletion would promote institutional simplicity and equal rules for all property owners, with no evident harm to the estate's value creation.

items.title: Appropriation Act 1921
items.summary: The Appropriation Act 1921 (c. 46) authorised the issue of public money from the Consolidated Fund and its appropriation for specified government services for the financial year ending 31 March 1922.
items.reason: This 1921 Act is entirely obsolete, having authorised expenditures over a century ago with no ongoing legal effect or obligations today. Keeping it on the statute books creates unnecessary clutter in the legal system, imposes maintenance costs on archives and databases, distorts legal research by bloating historical records, and sets a precedent for retaining defunct spending authorisations that perpetuate the illusion of unchecked government fiscal power without accountability. Deleting it incurs zero unseen costs while promoting a leaner, clearer legislative framework that reduces bureaucratic drag and aligns incentives toward scrutinising only live laws.

items.title: Safeguarding of Industries Act 1921
items.summary: The Safeguarding of Industries Act 1921 empowered the Board of Trade to impose tariffs and anti-dumping duties on imports competing with key British industries (e.g., matches, cutlery) deemed vital for defense or war-affected, to protect domestic production from foreign competition.
items.reason: This protectionist legislation distorts market incentives by shielding inefficient producers from competition, raising costs for consumers and downstream industries, reducing supply and innovation, and inviting trade retaliation. Unseen consequences include resource misallocation, rent-seeking by protected firms, and long-term decline in productivity, contradicting the liberty and private property foundations of wealth creation; British citizens would be better off with free trade fostering comparative advantages.

items.title: Corn Production Acts (Repeal) Act 1921
items.summary: The Corn Production Acts (Repeal) Act 1921 repeals the Corn Production Acts 1917 and related wartime legislation that imposed government controls, minimum prices, and subsidies on corn production.
items.reason: This act removed market-distorting interventions that propped up prices and controlled agricultural output, aligning with free-market principles by restoring liberty and private property in farming. Deleting it now risks reviving the original Corn Production Acts via the doctrine of repeal of a repealer, reimposing price controls and subsidies that would distort incentives, reduce supply efficiency, increase food costs, and harm prosperity—British citizens would be worse off without this safeguard against government overreach.

items.title: War Pensions Act 1921
items.summary: The War Pensions Act 1921 amends prior War Pensions Acts (1915-1920) by establishing local war pensions committees with mandated representation from veterans, widows, local authorities, employers, workers, and voluntary groups to advise on pension administration, handle complaints, distribute grants, and perform related duties; creates a central advisory committee; introduces final awards after four years, a seven-year claim limit, appeal modifications, pension commutation powers, and child care extensions.
items.reason: This act expands government bureaucracy through committees and ministerial oversight for administering time-limited WWI veteran pensions, imposing taxpayer-funded administrative costs, regulatory layers, and potential inefficiencies that distort incentives and create dependency precedents. Unseen consequences include persistent institutional bloat, lobbying for expansions beyond the 'present war,' and reduced private charity/voluntary efforts (already represented in committees). Private insurance, mutual aid, or charities could fulfill obligations more efficiently without state compulsion; the program's obsolescence (claims closed ~1928) means deletion incurs no harm but reduces dead statutory weight.

items.title: German Reparation (Recovery) Act 1921
items.summary: The German Reparation (Recovery) Act 1921 authorised the UK government to impose levies on German imports to recover post-WWI reparations owed under the Treaty of Versailles.
items.reason: This century-old act is obsolete, as German reparations were settled decades ago; retaining it clutters the statute book, risks misuse for protectionist trade barriers that distort incentives, raise consumer costs, and violate free trade principles central to prosperity, with no remaining benefits or enforcement relevance today.

items.title: Criminal Procedure (Scotland) Act 1921
items.summary: The Criminal Procedure (Scotland) Act 1921 reformed key aspects of Scottish criminal procedure, including provisions for appeals in solemn cases, summary trial procedures, and other judicial processes to ensure fairer handling of criminal matters.
items.reason: Deleting this foundational procedural framework would create gaps in Scotland's criminal justice system, leading to arbitrary state power, miscarriages of justice, and weakened rule of law; without it, fair trials, appeals, and due process protections—essential for safeguarding individual liberty against government overreach—would not reliably occur, leaving citizens vulnerable to unfair convictions and eroding the institutional stability needed for prosperity and property rights.

items.title: Education Act 1921
items.summary: The Education Act 1921 consolidated and amended prior education laws, regulating elementary education, local education authority powers, religious instruction, teacher appointments in voluntary schools, and financial support for non-provided schools.
items.reason: Government mandates on education enforce a state-dominated monopoly, crowding out private provision, distorting incentives for innovation, and imposing uniform standards that fail diverse needs. Unseen costs include higher taxes, bureaucratic inefficiency, suppressed competition leading to stagnation in quality, and perpetuation of dependency rather than individual choice; markets with private property would drive prosperity through tailored, efficient schooling.

items.title: Exchequer and Audit Departments Act 1921
items.summary: Amends 1866 and 1889 Exchequer and Audit Acts to strengthen independent examination by the Comptroller and Auditor General of government appropriation accounts, revenue receipts, cash, stock/store, and trading accounts; ensures spending matches parliamentary grants, reports irregularities to Parliament; sets deadlines, increases C&AG salary, adjusts staffing, and makes minor procedural changes.
items.reason: This establishes essential independent oversight of government spending and revenue collection, ensuring funds are used as Parliament intended and detecting unauthorized expenditure or procedural failures. Without it, self-auditing departments would lack credible checks, risking corruption, waste, and embezzlement—unseen costs like ballooning deficits or taxpayer-funded scandals that erode trust, increase effective taxation via inefficiency, and distort incentives for fiscal responsibility. Citizens would be worse off with reduced transparency and accountability over vast public funds, an institution that demonstrably curbs abuse absent market or private alternatives.

items.title: Expiring Laws Continuance Act 1921
items.summary: The Expiring Laws Continuance Act 1921 extends the validity of certain temporary Acts of Parliament scheduled to expire, typically for one year, allowing outdated wartime or emergency powers to persist without full legislative renewal.
items.reason: This Act exemplifies regulatory creep, where temporary measures evade proper sunset and become quasi-permanent without rigorous review, distorting incentives for lawmakers to repeal ineffective laws, bloating the statute book, and imposing unseen costs on liberty and economic efficiency by perpetuating government interventions long past their utility; modern needs should prompt explicit re-legislation if any provisions remain relevant, not reflexive continuance.

items.title: Public Works Loans Act 1921
items.summary: The Public Works Loans Act 1921 establishes and empowers the Public Works Loan Commissioners to provide loans to local authorities and public bodies for infrastructure and public works projects, financed through government borrowing.
items.reason: This legislation institutionalizes government-directed credit allocation, distorting private capital markets by subsidizing politically favored projects at below-market rates funded by national debt and taxpayer money. Unseen consequences include crowding out private investment (higher borrowing costs for businesses), moral hazard encouraging inefficient local spending and overinvestment in low-return public works, perpetuating bureaucracy, and long-term debt burdens that reduce prosperity and liberty without market discipline ensuring value creation.

items.title: Railways Act 1921
items.summary: The Railways Act 1921 reorganises Great Britain's railways by grouping 120+ companies into four amalgamated companies ('Big Four'), compensates for wartime government possession, establishes the Amalgamation Tribunal and Rates Tribunal to oversee mergers, standard charges (pegged to 1913 revenue), facilities, wages boards, and light railway procedures, imposing extensive state regulation on structure, pricing, labour, and operations.
items.reason: This Act exemplifies central planning by forcibly amalgamating private railways into government-sanctioned cartels, distorting property rights and competition; the Rates Tribunal enforces price controls tied to pre-war 'standard revenue', suppressing market pricing and innovation while creating regulatory capture; wage boards and service mandates interfere with voluntary contracts, raising costs and rigidities; unintended consequences include monopolistic inefficiencies, barriers to road competition, capital misallocation, and long-term decline (as seen in the Big Four's poor performance), harming wealth creation, liberty, and prosperity—markets would naturally consolidate efficient firms, spur efficiency via rivalry, and align supply with demand without bureaucratic overhead.

items.title: Supreme Court Officers (Retirement, Pensions, &c.) Act 1921
items.summary: The Supreme Court Officers (Retirement, Pensions, &c.) Act 1921 regulates retirement ages, pensions, and related employment conditions for administrative officers of the Supreme Court (historical UK court system).
items.reason: This century-old act enshrines rigid, special public sector pension and retirement rules that likely create generous entitlements funded by taxpayers, distorting labor markets by offering superior benefits unavailable in the private sector. Unintended consequences include fiscal burdens from unfunded liabilities, reduced incentives for efficiency in court administration, barriers to modernization or staff turnover, and crowding out of private investment. Deletion would allow market-driven employment contracts, aligning incentives with productivity and liberty without harming the essential rule of law, as core judicial functions can be supported through general employment laws.

items.title: Telegraph (Money) Act 1921
items.summary: The Telegraph (Money) Act 1921 was a short appropriation act authorizing public expenditure specifically for the operation and maintenance of the UK's telegraph services managed by the Post Office in the 1921-1922 fiscal period.
items.reason: This is an obsolete, one-off money bill for historical government spending on state-run telegraph services, which have long been privatized and technologically superseded (telegraphs are extinct). No ongoing effects or enforcement; retaining it bloats the statute book, increasing legal complexity, compliance costs for lawyers and businesses navigating archives, and distorting incentives by perpetuating a regulatory mindset favoring state intervention over market evolution. Unseen consequences include opportunity costs of time and resources better spent on productive activity, with zero benefit to modern prosperity or liberty.

items.title: Trusts (Scotland) Act 1921
items.summary: The Trusts (Scotland) Act 1921 consolidates and amends law on trusts in Scotland, granting trustees statutory powers to manage, sell, invest, and distribute trust property without court approval in routine cases, simplifying administration of private property arrangements.
items.reason: This legislation reduces transaction costs and legal uncertainties in managing private trusts, enabling efficient preservation and transfer of private property—a cornerstone of wealth creation and individualism. Without it, trustees would face higher burdens, more litigation, and delays, making citizens worse off by discouraging trust usage for estates, businesses, and philanthropy, outcomes not reliably achieved by common law alone.

items.title: Irish Railways (Settlement of Claims) Act 1921
items.summary: The Irish Railways (Settlement of Claims) Act 1921 (c. 59) provided a mechanism for settling specific historical claims related to railways in Ireland following the partition and Anglo-Irish Treaty.
items.reason: This is a century-old, one-off statute addressing claims from 1921 events, with provisions long since executed and no ongoing effect. Retaining it clogs the statute books, raises unseen costs in legal research and maintenance, distorts institutional clarity, and offers zero contribution to modern prosperity, property rights, or liberty—exemplifying regulatory bloat that Hayek warned crowds out productive activity.

items.title: Coal Mines (Decontrol) Act 1921
items.summary: The Coal Mines (Decontrol) Act 1921 ended wartime government control over the UK's coal mining industry, restoring private ownership and management.
items.reason: This was a one-time deregulation measure from 1921 whose effects have long been implemented and overtaken by later laws (nationalization in 1946, privatization in 1990s). Keeping it creates legislative clutter, raising unseen costs in legal research, compliance, and uncertainty for businesses and individuals navigating current law, without providing ongoing benefits or protections that markets and evolved property rights cannot sustain.

items.title: Shops (Early Closing) Act (1920) Amendment Act 1921
items.summary: Amendment to the Shops (Early Closing) Act 1920, regulating mandatory early closing times for shops to protect workers.
items.reason: Mandates shop closing hours, overriding private property rights and voluntary contracts between owners, workers, and consumers. Distorts retail incentives by limiting supply when demand exists (e.g., evenings/weekends), raising costs, reducing employment flexibility, and stifling competition; market-driven hours via wages/rest negotiations would better serve prosperity without unseen harms like bureaucracy and black-market workarounds.

items.title: Forestry Act 1921
items.summary: The Forestry Act 1921 established the Forestry Commission as a government body to promote afforestation, manage state forests, secure timber supplies, and regulate forestry activities, creating a centralised public sector monopoly over much of UK forestry.
items.reason: This act institutionalises government control over forestry, distorting private property incentives for landowners to invest in tree planting and timber production; it burdens taxpayers with funding inefficient bureaucratic management, crowds out private sector innovation in sustainable practices, and leads to suboptimal land use (e.g., prioritising non-commercial goals over market-driven timber supply, recreation, or carbon sequestration). Unseen consequences include suppressed timber supply increasing import reliance and costs, reduced adaptability to climate or market changes, and monopolistic stagnation harming long-term prosperity in rural economies.

items.title: Unemployed Workers' Dependants (Temporary Provisions) Act 1921
items.summary: Temporary 1921 Act providing allowances or benefits for dependants of unemployed workers during post-WWI economic distress.
items.reason: This outdated welfare intervention creates dependency, distorts labor market incentives by subsidizing unemployment, and imposes unseen fiscal costs through taxes or inflation that crowd out private charity and job creation; markets and voluntary mutual aid would better foster self-reliance and prosperity without government distortion.

items.title: Appropriation (No. 2) Act 1921
items.summary: Appropriation (No. 2) Act 1921 authorises the issue of specific sums from the Consolidated Fund for government expenditure in the fiscal year ending 31 March 1922.
items.reason: This is a spent, historical Act with no ongoing legal effect, as appropriation for that fiscal year has long been exhausted. Retaining obsolete legislation clutters the statute book, raises administrative costs for maintenance and access (e.g., PDF-only format), creates potential for legal confusion or erroneous reliance, and diverts resources from productive uses, contrary to principles of minimal government and clear incentives.

items.title: Poor Law Emergency Provisions (Scotland) Act 1921
items.summary: The Poor Law Emergency Provisions (Scotland) Act 1921 provided temporary emergency measures under Scotland's Poor Law system, likely for post-WWI relief amid economic distress, such as unemployment or hardship aid.
items.reason: This obsolete 1921 emergency welfare act, if still on the books, exemplifies government intervention that distorts labor incentives, fosters dependency, crowds out private charity, and burdens bureaucracy with unseen costs like moral hazard and reduced individual responsibility; modern prosperity requires deleting such relics to prioritize liberty and market-driven solutions over state relief that fails the poor long-term.

items.title: Trade Facilities Act 1921
items.summary: The Trade Facilities Act 1921 empowered the Treasury to guarantee up to 80% of bank loans to private enterprises for schemes approved as facilitating trade, export, or reconstruction post-WWI.
items.reason: Government loan guarantees distort credit allocation by overriding private risk assessment, fostering moral hazard (riskier lending on taxpayer-backed loans), cronyism in approving 'facilitating' schemes, and capital misallocation to politically favored projects over market-driven ones. Unseen costs include suppressed private investment, inflated costs from reduced competition, and entrenched precedent for future interventions that hinder entrepreneurship and prosperity.

items.title: National Health Insurance (Prolongation of Insurance) Act, 1921
items.summary: The National Health Insurance (Prolongation of Insurance) Act 1921 extended the duration of compulsory national health insurance coverage, originally established in 1911, likely as a temporary measure post-WWI to maintain benefits for insured workers amid economic uncertainty.
items.reason: This act exemplifies early government intervention in healthcare via compulsory insurance, distorting market incentives by forcing cross-subsidization, creating moral hazard (overuse of services), and reducing individual choice in providers and coverage. Unseen consequences include paving the way for the NHS monopoly, stifling private innovation, inflating costs through supply restrictions, and fostering dependency rather than prosperity. Now obsolete with the NHS since 1948, retaining it on the books incurs administrative deadweight, legal ambiguity, and symbolic endorsement of failed institutions that prioritize state control over liberty and efficiency—British citizens would gain clarity, reduced regulatory burden, and freer markets without it.

items.title: Local Authorities (Financial Provisions) Act 1921
items.summary: The Local Authorities (Financial Provisions) Act 1921 regulates borrowing, loans, sinking funds, and financial management for local authorities in England and Wales, enabling government entities to issue debt and manage funds in ways not subject to full market discipline.
items.reason: This act facilitates local government borrowing without private market risks, distorting incentives by allowing overspending, future tax burdens on citizens, and reduced accountability; unseen costs include crowding out private investment, perpetuating bureaucratic bloat, and hindering privatization of local services, as markets and voluntary arrangements would better allocate resources without state-enabled debt.

items.title: Tribunals of Inquiry (Evidence) Act 1921 (repealed)
items.summary: The Tribunals of Inquiry (Evidence) Act 1921 grants specific tribunals, established by parliamentary resolution for matters of urgent public importance, the powers of the High Court to summon witnesses, compel evidence, examine on oath, and punish contempt, while affording witnesses court-like immunities and regulating public access and representation.
items.reason: This Act enables effective, court-backed investigations into rare, parliament-approved matters of urgent public importance, such as major scandals or disasters, ensuring accountability without which key facts might remain hidden due to non-cooperation, eroding public trust, preventing institutional learning, and leaving citizens worse off by weakening oversight of government and public bodies; it imposes no ongoing regulatory burdens, market distortions, or unintended costs, functioning only ad hoc as a minimal institutional tool for liberty-preserving transparency.

items.title: Ministries of Munitions and Shipping (Cessation) Act 1921
items.summary: Act to formally dissolve the temporary Ministries of Munitions and Shipping, established during World War I, following the war's end.
items.reason: This 1921 Act served a one-time purpose of winding down wartime government departments that no longer exist. Retaining it on the statute books imposes unseen costs: cluttering the legal system, increasing administrative burdens for lawyers and officials parsing active law, distorting incentives by preserving historical interventions as precedents, and signaling persistent government overreach rather than a return to peacetime liberty and minimal state institutions. Deletion imposes zero costs, as the ministries ceased over a century ago, freeing resources for productive use.

items.title: Army and Air Force (Annual) Act 1921
items.summary: The Army and Air Force (Annual) Act 1921 annually authorised the maintenance of standing Army and Royal Air Force forces in peacetime, fulfilling constitutional requirements under the Bill of Rights 1689 for parliamentary consent.
items.reason: Obsolete and repealed/superseded by later legislation (e.g., Armed Forces Acts, now triennial). Keeping historical acts burdens the legal system with clutter, raising unseen costs like lawyer fees, compliance confusion for businesses/military, and distorted incentives via regulatory complexity that hampers entrepreneurship without delivering defence benefits.

items.title: Consolidated Fund (No. 1) Act 1922
items.summary: The Consolidated Fund (No. 1) Act 1922 authorizes the issue of specific sums from the UK's Consolidated Fund to meet government expenditure needs for the early part of the 1922-23 financial year, serving as a temporary measure until the full Appropriation Act.
items.reason: This is a century-old, temporary fiscal authorization act whose purpose was fulfilled upon issuance of the specified funds in 1922. It has no ongoing effect on current spending, which relies on annual modern equivalents. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: minor administrative burdens for maintenance on statute books, potential legal research confusion, and perpetuates clutter signaling tolerance for endless government fiscal interventions without sunset clauses. Deleting it streamlines the legal system without disrupting operations, aligning with institutional efficiency and skepticism of perpetual state mechanisms.

items.title: Kenya Divorces (Validity) Act 1922
items.summary: The Kenya Divorces (Validity) Act 1922 retroactively validated divorces granted in colonial Kenya, addressing jurisdictional recognition issues under UK law at the time.
items.reason: This 1922 Act addressed a narrow, historical colonial-era problem over a century ago; Kenya's independence in 1963 rendered it obsolete. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: cluttering the statute book increases legal complexity, research burdens for lawyers and citizens, and administrative overhead for government maintenance, distorting incentives by perpetuating irrelevant state intervention without achieving any modern outcomes. No British citizens would be worse off without it, as affected parties are long deceased and modern family law handles such matters.

items.title: Juries Act 1922
items.summary: The Juries Act 1922 governs the qualifications, summoning, selection, and procedures for juries in criminal and civil trials in England and Wales, establishing rules for jury composition, challenges, and service.
items.reason: Trial by jury is a cornerstone institution of English common law, protecting individual liberty by ensuring citizens are judged by peers rather than state-appointed judges alone, preventing government monopoly on justice and arbitrary power. Deleting this would erode this safeguard, leaving defendants vulnerable to judicial bias or overreach, with no market or private alternative emerging spontaneously to replicate it; historical evidence shows jury trials correlate with freer societies, and without statutory framework, ad hoc replacements would likely centralize power further, harming prosperity through weakened rule of law.

items.title: Representation of the People Act 1922
items.summary: The Representation of the People Act 1922 reformed the UK electoral system by abolishing plural voting (e.g., business premises and dual residence votes), redistributing parliamentary seats to better reflect population, lowering candidacy qualifications, and making other adjustments to promote fairer representation.
items.reason: Electoral institutions are foundational to liberty and preventing government capture by special interests; deleting this would risk reverting to malapportioned seats and plural voting that distort representation, enabling policies that harm prosperity through unaccountable rule; British citizens would be worse off with less democratic accountability and fairer competition for power, outcomes not spontaneously achieved without such frameworks.

items.title: Empire Settlement Act 1922
items.summary: The Empire Settlement Act 1922 provided for government-assisted migration from the UK to other parts of the British Empire, including financial aid, loans, and agreements with dominion governments to promote settlement and address UK unemployment.
items.reason: This act exemplifies government intervention in labor mobility, coercing taxpayers to subsidize relocation to imperial territories, distorting individual incentives and market-driven migration. It ignores that true prosperity comes from free markets and private property, not state-directed population transfers, which create dependency, misallocate resources, and fail to solve unemployment's root causes like regulatory burdens. The British Empire no longer exists in this form, rendering the act obsolete; its persistence imposes unseen administrative costs and symbolizes outdated central planning that hampers liberty and economic dynamism.

items.title: Audit (Local Authorities, etc.) Act 1922
items.summary: The Audit (Local Authorities, etc.) Act 1922 establishes requirements for the audit of accounts of local authorities and certain other public bodies by district auditors, ensuring financial transparency and accountability in the use of public funds.
items.reason: British citizens would be worse off without this legislation because local authorities manage vast taxpayer funds in a non-market environment prone to mismanagement and fraud; mandatory independent audits provide an essential institutional check that voluntary private arrangements are unlikely to replicate uniformly, preventing larger unseen losses from corruption and waste that outweigh compliance costs.

items.title: Government of the Soudan Loan (Amendment) Act 1922
items.summary: An Act amending legislation related to a loan from the UK Government to the Government of the Soudan (Sudan) in 1922.
items.reason: This 1922 amendment concerns a historical loan to the Anglo-Egyptian administered Soudan (now independent Sudan), long since settled or irrelevant post-decolonization. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute book, burdens legal research and maintenance, symbolizes outdated imperial entanglements, and diverts focus from modern institutions fostering prosperity. No British citizens would be worse off if deleted, as it imposes no active obligations, distorts no incentives, and creates no monopolies or supply reductions today.

items.title: Law of Property Act 1922
items.summary: The Law of Property Act 1922 is a comprehensive reform consolidating and modernising English land law. It defines legal estates (fee simple absolute, terms of years absolute, etc.), converts other interests to equitable ones, simplifies settlements via vesting and trust deeds, mandates trusts for sale for undivided shares, reforms mortgages to demises or legal charges, abolishes copyhold tenure (converting to freehold), extinguishes manorial incidents, converts perpetually renewable leaseholds to long terms, overhauls intestacy rules (trust for sale of realty, statutory next-of-kin shares), devolves realty to personal representatives, and updates Land Transfer Acts for registration.
items.reason: Deletion would revert to archaic, fragmented laws (e.g., Statute of Uses, copyhold, complex tenures) distorting property markets via high transaction costs, unclear titles, and feudal remnants, stifling alienability, investment, and wealth creation from private property. This Act establishes clear institutions for secure, transferable property rights, aligning with liberty and prosperity; without it, citizens face costlier conveyancing, reduced supply/competition in housing/land, monopolistic manorial burdens, harming individualism and growth. It achieves efficient outcomes via simplified rules, outweighing minor bureaucratic trusts.

items.title: Finance Act 1922
items.summary: The Finance Act 1922 implemented the UK's 1922 budget, setting rates and rules for income tax, corporation tax, customs and excise duties, stamp duties, and other fiscal measures, with many provisions since amended or repealed but some legacy elements potentially remaining.
items.reason: Retaining this century-old act imposes ongoing compliance costs, legal complexity, and uncertainty in a modern tax code that has long superseded it through subsequent Finance Acts; it distorts incentives via outdated fiscal structures, burdens private enterprise with historical regulatory bloat, and exemplifies how layered legislation creates unseen deadweight losses without delivering net benefits, as core tax functions are handled elsewhere.

items.title: Infanticide Act 1922
items.summary: The Infanticide Act 1922 provides that a mother who unlawfully kills her child under 12 months old, while her mind is disturbed by the effects of childbirth or lactation, may be convicted of the offence of infanticide (a felony with a maximum life sentence but often lesser penalties) rather than murder.
items.reason: This legislation recognizes diminished responsibility due to postpartum mental disturbance, a specific biological reality, allowing for proportionate justice without undermining the protection of infant life. Deleting it would force murder charges in cases of genuine mental impairment, leading to harsher sentences, potential miscarriages of justice, and distorted incentives in prosecution; common law alternatives lack this precise statutory defence, risking rigid application that harms mothers and erodes public trust in justice. Citizens would be worse off without it, as it achieves nuanced outcomes—protecting life while accounting for capacity—that voluntary institutions or general homicide laws cannot reliably replicate, with minimal regulatory costs or distortions.

items.title: Gaming Act 1922
items.summary: The Gaming Act 1922 imposed restrictions on commercial gaming houses, prohibiting certain forms of betting and gaming for money to curb gambling activities.
items.reason: This paternalistic legislation criminalizes voluntary exchanges between consenting adults, driving gambling underground into black markets rife with crime, violence, and fraud—unseen costs far exceeding any purported benefits. Enforcement diverts resources from productive uses, distorts incentives for safe market-provided gambling options, and erodes individual liberty and property rights without evidence of net societal gain, as markets self-regulate via reputation and competition.

items.title: Coroners (Emergency Provisions Continuance) Act 1922
items.summary: The Coroners (Emergency Provisions Continuance) Act 1922 extends temporary measures originally introduced during World War I to allow coroners greater flexibility in handling inquests, such as appointing deputies or simplifying procedures amid wartime disruptions.
items.reason: This 1922 continuance of WWI-era emergency powers is obsolete after over a century; modern coronial laws (e.g., Coroners and Justice Act 2009) have superseded it. Retaining it imposes unseen costs: legal uncertainty for practitioners researching archives, bloated statute books distorting incentives for efficient lawmaking, and potential for misguided reliance in crises. No evidence it achieves ongoing benefits; deletion promotes institutional clarity, reduces compliance burdens, and frees resources without harming public safety or liberty.

items.title: Indian High Courts Act 1922
items.summary: The Indian High Courts Act 1922 (c. 20) was enacted to amend provisions relating to the constitution, jurisdiction, and powers of High Courts in British India.
items.reason: This legislation is obsolete following Indian independence in 1947 and the end of British colonial rule; it has no application to modern UK jurisdiction or institutions. Keeping it on the statute books imposes unseen costs including cluttering the legal corpus, increasing research burdens for lawyers and citizens, perpetuating irrelevant colonial-era text, and distorting focus from active laws without delivering any benefits to liberty, property, or prosperity.

items.title: Treaties of Washington Act 1922
items.summary: The Treaties of Washington Act 1922 (c. 21) ratified international agreements from the 1921-1922 Washington Naval Conference, including the Five-Power Naval Treaty limiting naval armaments, the Four-Power Treaty on Pacific possessions, and the Nine-Power Treaty on China, to promote disarmament and stability post-WWI.
items.reason: This Act is obsolete; the treaties it ratified were superseded by later agreements (e.g., London Naval Treaty 1930), abrogated during WWII, or irrelevant to modern geopolitics and technology. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute book, risks legal misinterpretation in historical disputes, diverts parliamentary resources from current issues, and perpetuates government overreach by maintaining defunct international commitments that distort incentives for defense policy without delivering prosperity or liberty benefits today.

items.title: Summer Time Act 1922
items.summary: The Summer Time Act 1922 introduced and regulates British Summer Time (BST), mandating the advancement of clocks by one hour during summer months (originally April to October) to extend evening daylight.
items.reason: Mandating uniform clock changes across the UK distorts individual and business incentives, imposing one-size-fits-all scheduling that harms sectors like agriculture and early-morning operations while causing unseen costs such as increased accidents, sleep disruption, and health issues post-change; studies show negligible or negative net energy savings, and private markets or voluntary local adoption could achieve any benefits without government coercion, freeing individuals to align time with natural preferences and productivity.

items.title: Harbours, Docks and Piers (Temporary Increase of Charges) Act 1922
items.summary: Permits a temporary increase in charges levied at harbours, docks, and piers.
items.reason: Government-mandated price controls distort market pricing mechanisms, preventing harbours from responding to supply, demand, and costs via private property rights. Retaining this 1922-era intervention perpetuates regulatory precedent, creates uncertainty for port investments, raises unseen costs to shipping and trade by discouraging efficiency and competition, and undermines UK maritime prosperity—outcomes better achieved through free markets without state decree.

items.title: Government of Nothern Ireland (Loan Guarantee) Act 1922
items.summary: Authorises the UK Treasury to guarantee payment of principal and interest on loans up to £3.5 million raised by the Government of Northern Ireland, subject to NI securing repayment from its Consolidated Fund and provisions for UK reimbursement, with parliamentary reporting.
items.reason: This one-off 1922 guarantee creates moral hazard by subsidising NI borrowing with UK taxpayer risk, distorting incentives for fiscal prudence and fostering inter-government dependency rather than market discipline. Even if obsolete, it sets a precedent for bailouts that crowd out private investment, inflates public debt, and burdens future generations with unseen costs; deletion enforces responsibility without evident benefits today.

items.title: British Empire Exhibition (Amendment) Act 1922
items.summary: An Act amending the legislation authorizing the British Empire Exhibition, a one-time promotional event held at Wembley in 1924-1925 to showcase British Empire trade and culture.
items.reason: This is obsolete legislation for a century-old temporary event with no ongoing application or enforcement. Keeping it on the books creates unseen costs: administrative burden on legal maintenance, confusion in statute searches, and dilution of the legal system's clarity, distorting incentives for efficient governance without providing any benefits to liberty, property, or prosperity today.

items.title: Anglo-Persian Oil Company (Payment of Calls) Act 1922
items.summary: The Anglo-Persian Oil Company (Payment of Calls) Act 1922 (c. 26) is a specific statute regulating or compelling the payment of calls (likely on shares) by the Anglo-Persian Oil Company, predecessor to BP.
items.reason: This 1922 act addressed a one-off financial matter for a long-defunct entity; any obligations were fulfilled decades ago. Keeping it imposes unseen costs: clutters the statute book, confuses legal researchers and practitioners who must verify its irrelevance, distorts incentives by preserving bureaucratic inertia in law maintenance, and exemplifies government overreach into private company affairs without ongoing benefit to liberty or prosperity.

items.title: Canals (Continuance of Charging Powers) Act 1922
items.summary: Act extending the duration of charging powers (tolls and fees) granted to canal owners or authorities under prior legislation.
items.reason: This 1922 act perpetuates time-limited government-granted permissions for canal operators to charge users, an intervention that undermines perpetual private property rights. Canals, now mostly leisure assets managed by trusts, face distorted incentives: reliance on statutory powers discourages market-based pricing, innovation, or privatization. Unseen costs include legal uncertainty, compliance burdens, and precedent for ongoing state meddling in infrastructure, stifling entrepreneurship in transport alternatives. Deletion restores liberty for owners to contract freely without 100-year-old crutches.

items.title: Bread Acts Amendment Act 1922
items.summary: The Bread Acts Amendment Act 1922 amends earlier Bread Acts regulating the production, weighing, standardization, and sale of bread to prevent fraud such as short-weight loaves and adulteration.
items.reason: These regulations imposed government-mandated standards on bread weight, ingredients, and baking processes, ostensibly to protect consumers, but distorted market incentives by raising compliance costs for small bakers, creating barriers to entry, favoring large producers, and stifling innovation in products like sliced or specialty breads. Competition, private reputation, and voluntary contracts would better ensure quality without state intervention, as evidenced by modern bakery markets thriving post-repeal. Unseen costs include higher bread prices, reduced supply variety, and entrepreneurial suppression, hindering prosperity without commensurate benefits today.

items.title: Sale of Tea Act 1922
items.summary: The Sale of Tea Act 1922 prohibits the sale of tea for human consumption unless it consists exclusively of the leaves of the tea plant (Camellia sinensis), aiming to prevent adulteration with other substances.
items.reason: This 1922 regulation imposes a narrow product definition that distorts market incentives for innovation in tea products and blends, raises compliance costs for sellers through bureaucratic enforcement, and creates unseen consequences like reduced supply variety and higher prices. In a free market, competition, branding, voluntary labeling, and general fraud/contract laws would better protect consumers by rewarding quality and punishing deceivers, without government overreach stifling trade; the act is now redundant amid broader modern food safety frameworks.

items.title: Consolidated Fund (No. 2) Act 1922
items.summary: An Act authorizing the issue of sums from the Consolidated Fund for public services in 1922, likely a short-term appropriation measure now obsolete.
items.reason: This 1922 fiscal authorization has long expired, with its spending powers superseded by modern Consolidated Fund and Appropriation Acts. Retaining it clutters the statute book, fostering legal complexity, higher compliance costs for citizens and businesses navigating obsolete laws, and signaling bureaucratic inertia that distorts incentives for efficient governance. Deletion incurs no unseen costs, as no current expenditures rely on it, and simplifies institutions without harming public services.

items.title: Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Act 1922
items.summary: The Unemployment Insurance (No. 2) Act 1922 extended unemployment insurance coverage to additional trades (e.g., agriculture, domestic service), increased benefits, and raised contributions amid 1920s economic hardship, building on the 1920 Act to compel employer-employee state insurance.
items.reason: Mandatory state insurance distorts labor markets by imposing payroll taxes that raise hiring costs and reduce employment; creates moral hazard encouraging job loss or riskier behavior; fosters dependency culture over self-reliance and private mutual aid societies; unintended consequences include bureaucratic expansion into modern welfare state's fiscal burdens (trillions in costs, disincentivizing work, crowding out private provision), preventing wealth creation via free markets and individualism as Mises/Hayek/Friedman warned.

items.title: Universities (Scotland) Act 1922
items.summary: The Universities (Scotland) Act 1922 extends powers of Scottish university courts to make ordinances on age limits for principals/professors' tenure, pensions/superannuation systems, and admission of lecturers/readers to the Senatus Academicus; also automatically grants general council membership to lecturers/readers after one year.
items.reason: Deletion would rigidify university governance under outdated 1889 rules, preventing efficient retirement of underperforming senior staff, modernization of pensions (potentially leading to funding shortfalls or retention of deadwood), and inclusion of junior academics in decision-making, harming institutional adaptability and educational quality without state intervention—outcomes not self-emerging in rigid systems lacking such targeted autonomy.

items.title: Appropriation Act 1922
items.summary: The Appropriation Act 1922 (c. 32) authorises the issue from the Consolidated Fund of specific sums for government services in the fiscal years ending 31 March 1922 and 1923, appropriating them to purposes detailed in schedules.
items.reason: This is a one-time, historical spending authorisation for fiscal years a century ago, almost certainly repealed or superseded by later Appropriation Acts and Consolidated Fund Acts. Keeping obsolete statutes on the books imposes unseen costs: legal researchers must verify irrelevance, archives incur maintenance expenses, and clutter risks misinterpretation or false reliance, distorting incentives for efficient governance without providing any ongoing benefit to liberty, property, or prosperity.

items.title: Public Works Loans Act 1922
